lectures.title: Lecture 1: Endo1_2: Overview of the Endocrine System & Neuroendocrine Systems

questions.q: Ernest Starling (1905) defined hormones as chemical messengers that coordinate activities of different parts of the body. Which of the following is NOT one of the three key properties of a classical hormone as defined by Starling?
options: Secreted into the blood in very small amounts by specialized cells or glands
Carried to other parts of the body by the bloodstream
Interacts with specific receptors in target tissue cells to produce a biological response
Stored in vesicles within target cells until needed for a response
Acts on tissues distant from the site of secretion
questions.explain: Starling's three properties are: (1) secreted into blood in small amounts, (2) carried by the bloodstream, and (3) interacts with specific receptors in target cells. Storage in target cell vesicles is not part of Starling's definition — hormones are synthesized and secreted by specialized glands, not stored by target cells. Option E is implicit in property 2 and is not a separate defining criterion in Starling's original formulation.

questions.q: Which of the following newly recognized endocrine organs produces atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP), renin, and calcitriol — correctly matched to their respective sources?
options: ANP: heart; renin: kidney; calcitriol: kidney
ANP: kidney; renin: heart; calcitriol: liver
ANP: liver; renin: kidney; calcitriol: adrenal gland
ANP: adipose tissue; renin: heart; calcitriol: bone
ANP: stomach; renin: liver; calcitriol: pancreas
questions.explain: The heart secretes ANP (atrial natriuretic peptide), while the kidneys secrete both renin and calcitriol. Students often confuse ANP with renal hormones since ANP acts on the kidney, but its origin is cardiac. The liver secretes somatomedin (IGF), not calcitriol. Option A has ANP and renin sources swapped.

questions.q: GLP-1 (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1) exerts multiple physiological effects through its G protein-coupled receptor. Which combination of effects is correct?
options: Enhances glucagon release, promotes satiety, and accelerates gastric emptying
Inhibits insulin secretion, promotes satiety, and slows gastric emptying
Enhances insulin secretion, inhibits glucagon release, slows gastric emptying, and promotes satiety
Inhibits insulin secretion, inhibits glucagon release, and slows gastric emptying
Enhances insulin secretion, promotes glucagon release, and accelerates gastric emptying
questions.explain: GLP-1 acts on a GPCR to enhance insulin secretion, inhibit glucagon release, slow gastric emptying, and promote satiety — all four effects together make it a major drug target for type 2 diabetes and obesity. Option A incorrectly states glucagon enhancement and gastric acceleration. Options B and D incorrectly state that GLP-1 inhibits insulin. Option E has both insulin and glucagon directions wrong.

questions.q: Which of the three types of stimuli that regulate hormone secretion applies when low blood Ca²⁺ levels trigger release of parathyroid hormone?
options: Neural stimuli, because the nervous system detects ionic changes in blood
Hormonal stimuli, because Ca²⁺ acts as a second messenger for upstream hormones
Humoral changes, because blood composition (Ca²⁺ concentration) directly triggers secretion
Paracrine stimuli, because adjacent parathyroid cells detect the ionic change locally
Autocrine stimuli, because parathyroid cells self-regulate their own secretion
questions.explain: Humoral changes refer to alterations in blood composition — such as changes in Ca²⁺, blood glucose, or osmolarity — that directly stimulate hormone secretion. Parathyroid hormone release in response to low blood Ca²⁺ is the classic humoral example. Neural stimuli involve nerve impulses (e.g., sympathetic control of adrenal medulla), and hormonal stimuli involve upstream hormones controlling downstream gland secretion.

questions.q: The endocrine and nervous systems can use the same molecule to transmit signals. Which of the following molecules serves as BOTH a neurotransmitter and a hormone?
options: Cortisol
Oxytocin
Norepinephrine
Thyroxine
Estradiol
questions.explain: Norepinephrine (去甲腎上腺素) serves as a neurotransmitter in the sympathetic nervous system and as a hormone when secreted by the adrenal medulla into the bloodstream. Cortisol, thyroxine, and estradiol are exclusively hormones; oxytocin functions as a hormone (from posterior pituitary) and as a neurotransmitter in the brain — but norepinephrine is the canonical lecture example for this dual role.

questions.q: Which hormone is an amino acid derivative but is classified as lipid-soluble and acts via nuclear receptors — unlike the other amino acid derivative hormones?
options: Epinephrine, derived from tyrosine
Melatonin, derived from tryptophan
Norepinephrine, derived from tyrosine
Thyroxine, derived from tyrosine
Serotonin, derived from tryptophan
questions.explain: Thyroxine is derived from tyrosine (an amino acid) but is lipid-soluble and acts via intracellular/nuclear receptors — making it the notable exception among amino acid derivatives. Epinephrine and norepinephrine (also tyrosine-derived) are lipid-insoluble and use surface receptors. Melatonin (tryptophan-derived) is also lipid-insoluble. This exception is frequently tested.

questions.q: Insulin is first synthesized as preproinsulin, then processed to proinsulin, and finally cleaved to insulin with two polypeptide chains. Which chemical class of hormones does this processing exemplify, and what general rule does it illustrate?
options: Steroids; all steroid hormones undergo enzymatic cleavage from a cholesterol precursor
Eicosanoids; prostaglandins are all first synthesized as larger arachidonic-acid-based precursors
Amino acid derivatives; tyrosine-derived hormones are always first made as larger precursors
Polypeptides; they are often produced as larger precursor molecules that are proteolytically cleaved to the active form
Prostaglandins; phospholipase cleavage of membrane lipids yields the active hormone directly
questions.explain: Polypeptide hormones are often synthesized as larger molecular weight precursors (preprohormones → prohormones → active hormone) that are proteolytically cleaved to generate the active form. Insulin is a classic example. Steroids are derived from cholesterol by enzymatic modification, not proteolytic cleavage of a protein precursor. Amino acid derivative hormones like thyroxine and epinephrine are not made as large protein precursors in the same way.

questions.q: Which of the following is the key structural feature shared by ALL steroid hormones?
options: All are derived from arachidonic acid and contain a 20-carbon polyunsaturated chain
All are derived from cholesterol and are lipid-soluble
All are derived from tyrosine and contain an aromatic ring structure
All contain a disulfide bond linking two polypeptide chains
All are derived from tryptophan and contain an indole ring
questions.explain: All steroid hormones are derived from cholesterol and are lipid-soluble. Differences in their ring structure and side chains create functional diversity (e.g., cortisol, aldosterone, testosterone, estradiol). Arachidonic acid is the precursor for prostaglandins/eicosanoids, not steroids. Tyrosine yields amino acid derivative hormones like epinephrine and thyroxine; tryptophan yields melatonin.

questions.q: Prostaglandins are lipid-soluble but do NOT act like other lipid-soluble hormones. Which of the following correctly describes how their mechanism of action differs from other lipid-soluble hormones such as steroids?
options: Prostaglandins enter the nucleus and bind to different nuclear receptor subtypes than steroids, modifying different gene sets
Prostaglandins bypass receptors entirely and directly activate ion channels in the plasma membrane
Prostaglandins are packaged into lipoproteins that deliver them directly into the cytoplasm of target cells
Prostaglandins act exclusively through receptor tyrosine kinases, whereas steroids use nuclear receptors
Prostaglandins bind to surface receptors coupled with cAMP signaling, rather than to intracellular nuclear receptors
questions.explain: Despite being lipid-soluble, prostaglandins bind to surface receptors (GPCRs) coupled with cAMP signaling — behaving like lipid-insoluble hormones in terms of their signaling mechanism. This is the opposite of what would be expected for a lipid-soluble molecule. Steroids and thyroxine, by contrast, diffuse across the membrane to bind intracellular/nuclear receptors. This distinction is one of the most commonly tested points in this lecture.

questions.q: NSAIDs (non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs) such as aspirin and ibuprofen reduce fever, pain, and inflammation. These drugs are also being investigated for treating Alzheimer's disease. What is the mechanism by which NSAIDs exert their anti-inflammatory effects?
options: They block G protein-coupled surface receptors for prostaglandins, preventing receptor activation
They directly inhibit phospholipase enzymes, blocking arachidonic acid release from membranes
They compete with prostaglandins for binding to intracellular nuclear receptors in immune cells
They inhibit enzymes involved in prostaglandin synthesis, reducing the formation of prostaglandins
They chelate calcium ions in the blood, preventing prostaglandin-mediated inflammatory cell activation
questions.explain: NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes involved in prostaglandin biosynthesis, reducing the formation of prostaglandins that mediate fever, pain, and inflammation. They do not block prostanoid surface receptors (that would be receptor antagonism) nor do they act on nuclear receptors (prostaglandins use surface receptors). Option B describes blocking phospholipase — a different step in the pathway upstream of COX.

questions.q: Elwood Jensen first described the estrogen receptor in 1958 and later discovered the superfamily of nuclear hormone receptors. Which class of hormones uses these nuclear receptors to produce its effects?
options: Lipid-soluble hormones including steroids and thyroxine, which diffuse across the membrane and bind intracellular receptors that act on DNA
Polypeptide hormones that bind surface GPCRs and activate cAMP second messengers
Amino acid derivatives that bind surface receptors with intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity
Prostaglandins that bind surface receptors coupled to cGMP production
All hormones, because every hormone class contains members that use nuclear receptors
questions.explain: Lipid-soluble hormones (steroids and thyroxine) diffuse freely across the plasma membrane and bind to intracellular receptors in the nucleus or cytoplasm. The hormone-receptor complex then acts directly on DNA to modify gene transcription, producing long-lasting effects (hours to days). Polypeptides and most amino acid derivatives use surface receptors. Prostaglandins are lipid-soluble but exceptionally use surface cAMP-coupled receptors, not nuclear receptors.

questions.q: A researcher is studying cortisol, a lipid-soluble steroid hormone. To prepare a 100 mM stock solution for cell culture experiments, which solvent should be used?
options: Ethanol, because cortisol is a lipid-soluble steroid that does not dissolve in aqueous solutions
Distilled water, because it is the most physiologically neutral and widely used lab solvent
Saline (0.9% NaCl), because it best mimics the ionic composition of extracellular fluid
Acidic buffer at pH 5, because cortisol solubility increases substantially under acidic conditions
Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), because it maintains pH and prevents protein denaturation
questions.explain: Cortisol is a lipid-soluble steroid hormone and does not dissolve in water-based solvents such as distilled water, saline, or PBS. An organic solvent such as ethanol must be used to prepare a concentrated stock solution, which is then diluted 1000-fold into aqueous cell culture media. This is a direct practical application of the principle that lipid-soluble hormones are insoluble in water — a quiz question explicitly given in the lecture.

questions.q: Which of the following is a key distinguishing feature of lipid-insoluble hormone signaling compared to lipid-soluble hormone signaling?
options: Lipid-insoluble hormones bind surface receptors and produce rapid, short-lived responses via second messengers or catalytic receptor activity
Lipid-insoluble hormones produce long-lasting effects lasting hours to days due to transcriptional changes
Lipid-insoluble hormones enter the nucleus by active transport and directly modify DNA transcription
Lipid-insoluble hormones dissolve in the lipid bilayer and gate ion channels from within the membrane
Lipid-insoluble hormones always trigger apoptosis in target cells as part of their short-lived signaling
questions.explain: Lipid-insoluble hormones (polypeptides and most amino acid derivatives) bind surface receptors and produce rapid, short-lived responses mediated by second messengers (like cAMP or IP3/DAG) or intrinsic catalytic activity of receptors (like tyrosine kinases). Lipid-soluble hormones, not lipid-insoluble ones, produce long-lasting effects via nuclear receptor-DNA interactions. Both GPCRs and enzyme-coupled receptors can mediate lipid-insoluble hormone signaling.

questions.q: Epinephrine can both stimulate and inhibit adenylate cyclase depending on the receptor subtype it binds. Which pairing correctly identifies the receptor subtypes and their effects on cAMP?
options: Alpha-1 adrenoceptors stimulate adenylate cyclase; beta-adrenoceptors inhibit it
Alpha-2 adrenoceptors stimulate adenylate cyclase; beta-adrenoceptors inhibit it
Beta-adrenoceptors stimulate adenylate cyclase; alpha-1 adrenoceptors inhibit it
Dopamine receptors stimulate adenylate cyclase; beta-adrenoceptors inhibit it
Beta-adrenoceptors stimulate adenylate cyclase; alpha-2 adrenoceptors inhibit it
questions.explain: Epinephrine activates adenylate cyclase (stimulatory cAMP signaling) via beta-adrenoceptors, and inhibits adenylate cyclase via alpha-2 adrenoceptors. Alpha-1 adrenoceptors use the IP3/DAG pathway rather than cAMP (so they are not directly involved in adenylate cyclase regulation). This bidirectional regulation by the same molecule through different GPCR subtypes is a high-yield exam concept.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of the insulin receptor?
options: It has intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity that is activated by insulin binding
It phosphorylates tyrosine residues on cytoplasmic proteins when activated by insulin
It autophosphorylates upon ligand binding, creating a positive feedback increase in kinase activity
It binds a water-soluble polypeptide ligand at its extracellular domain
It is coupled to a G protein that activates adenylate cyclase to generate cAMP
questions.explain: The insulin receptor is a receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK), NOT a GPCR. It is NOT coupled to G proteins or adenylate cyclase. It has intrinsic kinase activity, phosphorylates cytoplasmic tyrosine residues, and autophosphorylates (positive feedback). Insulin itself is water-soluble and binds the extracellular domain. Confusing insulin's receptor with GPCR signaling is the most common exam error on this topic.

questions.q: The ANP (atrial natriuretic peptide) receptor differs fundamentally from both GPCRs and receptor tyrosine kinases in the type of enzymatic activity it possesses. Which of the following correctly describes the ANP receptor?
options: It is a receptor adenylate cyclase that generates cAMP upon ANP binding
It is a receptor guanylate cyclase that generates cGMP upon ANP binding
It is a receptor tyrosine kinase that phosphorylates tyrosine residues on cytoplasmic proteins
It is a ligand-gated ion channel that allows Ca²⁺ influx upon ANP binding
It is a nuclear receptor that ANP enters the cell to bind, modifying gene transcription
questions.explain: The ANP receptor is a receptor guanylate cyclase — its extracellular domain binds ANP, and its intracellular domain has guanylate cyclase activity that converts GTP to cGMP. This is distinct from adenylate cyclase (cAMP, used by many GPCRs) and receptor tyrosine kinases (phosphotyrosine signals). The cGMP produced mediates ANP's vasodilatory and natriuretic effects. ANP is a peptide hormone and cannot cross the membrane to use nuclear receptors.

questions.q: Neurosecretory (neuroendocrine) cells differ structurally from ordinary neurons. Which of the following correctly describes a structural feature unique to neurosecretory cells?
options: They contain synaptic vesicles (30–60 nm) identical to those of ordinary neurons but in greater numbers
They contain secretory granules (100–400 nm) that are much larger than the synaptic vesicles (30–60 nm) of ordinary neurons
They lack axons and release hormones by passive diffusion from the cell body
They are non-excitable and lack voltage-gated ion channels required for action potentials
They form conventional synapses on postsynaptic neurons and secrete hormones only at these synaptic terminals
questions.explain: Neurosecretory cells contain secretory granules of 100–400 nm, significantly larger than the synaptic vesicles (30–60 nm) found in ordinary neurons. They terminate near capillary beds (the neurohemal organ) rather than at conventional synapses. They ARE excitable neurons — stating they are non-excitable (option D) is explicitly FALSE per the lecture.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about neurosecretory cells in the hypothalamus is FALSE?
options: They are specialized neurons that release hormones called neurohormones
They respond to sensory inputs from other parts of the body like ordinary neurons
They form an interface between the neural and endocrine systems
Chromaffin cells of the adrenal medulla are a type of neurosecretory cell sharing neural embryological origin
They are non-excitable cells that release hormones by passive diffusion rather than action potentials
questions.explain: Neurosecretory cells ARE excitable neurons — they can be depolarized and fire action potentials, which triggers regulated hormone release by exocytosis. The statement that they are 'non-excitable' is explicitly false per the lecture quiz. They secrete neurohormones, respond to sensory inputs, form a neuroendocrine interface, and chromaffin cells of the adrenal medulla share neural embryological origin.

questions.q: Magnocellular neurons project to the posterior pituitary, while parvocellular neurons project to the portal capillaries. What is the primary functional difference between these two populations of hypothalamic neurosecretory neurons?
options: Magnocellular neurons release CRH and TRH into portal blood; parvocellular neurons release ADH and oxytocin into systemic blood
Magnocellular neurons are larger and control growth hormone; parvocellular neurons are smaller and control reproductive hormones
Both neuron types release identical neurohormones but the destination (posterior pituitary vs. portal system) determines the hormonal effect
Magnocellular neurons synthesize releasing hormones for the anterior pituitary; parvocellular neurons synthesize neurohormones for systemic release

Magnocellular neurons synthesize and release ADH and oxytocin (stored in posterior pituitary) into systemic blood; parvocellular neurons release releasing/inhibiting hormones into the portal system to control the anterior pituitary
questions.explain: Magnocellular neurons (in SON and PVN) synthesize ADH (vasopressin) and oxytocin, stored and released from the posterior pituitary into systemic circulation. Parvocellular neurons release hypothalamic releasing and release-inhibiting hormones into the hypophyseal portal capillaries to control anterior pituitary glandular hormone secretion. Option A has the two populations' hormones and destinations reversed.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly distinguishes the anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis) from the posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis) in terms of tissue type and function?
options: Anterior pituitary: neural tissue (axon terminals of hypothalamic neurons); synthesizes ADH and oxytocin
Anterior pituitary: true endocrine glandular tissue; synthesizes glandular hormones such as GH, prolactin, and ACTH
Posterior pituitary: true endocrine tissue; synthesizes releasing hormones for the hypothalamus
Posterior pituitary: glandular endocrine tissue; synthesizes TSH, FSH, and LH
Both lobes are identical in tissue type; the difference is only in which bloodstream the hormones drain into
questions.explain: The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis) is true endocrine glandular tissue and synthesizes its own glandular hormones (GH, prolactin, MSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, TSH). The posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis) is neural tissue — composed of axon terminals of magnocellular hypothalamic neurons — and only stores and releases ADH and oxytocin, which are actually synthesized in SON and PVN of the hypothalamus.

questions.q: The hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal system is defined as two capillary beds directly joined by portal blood vessels. What physiological advantage does this vascular arrangement provide?
options: It enables the posterior pituitary to receive releasing hormones from the hypothalamus at high concentrations
It dilutes hypothalamic neurohormones before they enter systemic circulation, protecting the body from high concentrations
It delivers hypothalamic releasing and inhibiting hormones to the anterior pituitary at high local concentrations, ensuring effective control without systemic exposure
It allows the anterior pituitary to recycle hormones back to the hypothalamus for feedback inhibition via direct blood flow
It concentrates ADH and oxytocin in the posterior pituitary by countercurrent exchange between the two capillary beds
questions.explain: The portal system delivers parvocellular-released hormones directly from hypothalamic capillaries to the anterior pituitary at high local concentrations through two capillary beds joined by portal vessels, before they reach systemic blood where they would be diluted. This ensures effective control of anterior pituitary secretion without requiring systemically high concentrations of hypothalamic hormones. The posterior pituitary is not connected via the portal system — it receives its hormones directly from axon terminals of magnocellular neurons.

questions.q: Which of the following is a direct-acting anterior pituitary hormone that acts on somatic, non-endocrine target tissues rather than on other endocrine glands?
options: ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone), which stimulates the adrenal cortex
TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone), which stimulates the thyroid gland
LH (luteinizing hormone), which stimulates the gonads
FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone), which stimulates ovarian follicles
Prolactin, which stimulates milk protein synthesis in mammary glands
questions.explain: Prolactin is a direct-acting anterior pituitary hormone that acts on somatic non-endocrine targets such as the mammary gland (stimulating milk protein synthesis and mammary gland growth). ACTH, TSH, FSH, and LH are all tropic hormones — they act on other endocrine glands (adrenal cortex, thyroid, and gonads respectively) to stimulate hormone secretion. GH and MSH are also direct-acting hormones.

questions.q: Prolactin secretion is regulated by inhibitory and stimulatory inputs. Which of the following correctly identifies the inhibitory and stimulatory regulators of prolactin release?
options: Inhibited by dopamine; stimulated by TRH and estrogen
Inhibited by serotonin; stimulated by TSH and progesterone
Inhibited by TRH; stimulated by dopamine and estrogen
Inhibited by estrogen; stimulated by dopamine and TRH
Inhibited by FSH; stimulated by LH and prolactin-releasing factor only
questions.explain: Prolactin is inhibited by dopamine (also called prolactin-inhibiting hormone) and stimulated by TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone) and estrogen. This is why dopamine agonists (e.g., bromocriptine) reduce prolactin levels, and why hyperprolactinemia can occur in hypothyroidism (elevated TRH stimulates prolactin). Option B has the inhibitory and stimulatory roles of dopamine and TRH reversed.

questions.q: Both MSH and ACTH are derived from pro-opiomelanocortin (POMC). Which of the following correctly explains the mechanism of skin darkening in Addison's disease?
options: Addison's disease damages the adrenal medulla, causing excess epinephrine that directly stimulates melanocytes to produce melanin
Addison's disease reduces cortisol production, eliminating negative feedback on CRH, which rises and drives increased POMC cleavage, elevating both ACTH and MSH, causing hyperpigmentation
Addison's disease increases cortisol production, which stimulates POMC cleavage and raises MSH levels, causing skin darkening
Addison's disease damages the anterior pituitary, reducing ACTH and MSH equally, causing pale rather than darkened skin
Addison's disease elevates estrogen, which directly stimulates melanocyte production of melanin at the receptor level
questions.explain: In Addison's disease, destruction of the adrenal cortex reduces cortisol. Without cortisol's negative feedback, hypothalamic CRH rises. High CRH drives increased POMC processing in the anterior pituitary, simultaneously elevating both ACTH and MSH. The elevated MSH stimulates melanocytes in the skin, causing characteristic hyperpigmentation. Addison's disease damages the adrenal cortex, not the adrenal medulla or pituitary.

questions.q: According to the lecture mnemonic about hypothalamic releasing and release-inhibiting hormones, which of the following statements is correct?
options: Both direct-acting and tropic hormones have releasing hormones AND release-inhibiting hormones from the hypothalamus
Only tropic hormones have hypothalamic releasing hormones; direct-acting hormones are not regulated by the hypothalamus
All direct-acting and tropic hormones have releasing hormones, but ONLY direct-acting hormones also have release-inhibiting hormones
All anterior pituitary hormones have release-inhibiting hormones, but only tropic hormones have releasing hormones
MSH has a unique releasing hormone entirely separate from the CRH pathway, because it comes from pars intermedia
questions.explain: The lecture mnemonic: all direct-acting (GH, prolactin, MSH) and tropic (ACTH, FSH, LH, TSH) hormones have corresponding releasing hormones from hypothalamic parvocellular neurons. However, only direct-acting hormones also have release-inhibiting hormones (e.g., somatostatin for GH, dopamine for prolactin). Tropic hormones lack release-inhibiting counterparts. MSH and ACTH share CRH as the main hypothalamic signal because both are derived from POMC.

questions.q: ADH (antidiuretic hormone/vasopressin) acts on two distinct target organs through different mechanisms. Which pairing correctly matches each target to its mechanism of action?
options: Kidney: vasoconstriction via IP3/DAG; Vascular smooth muscle: aquaporin insertion via cAMP
Kidney: direct vasoconstriction to increase filtration; Vascular smooth muscle: aquaporin upregulation
Kidney: water excretion via aquaporin blocking; Vascular smooth muscle: vasodilation to lower blood pressure
Kidney: aquaporin upregulation via adenylate cyclase (cAMP) to promote water retention; Vascular smooth muscle: direct vasoconstriction to regulate blood pressure
Kidney: cGMP-mediated diuresis via receptor guanylate cyclase; Vascular smooth muscle: cAMP-mediated vasoconstriction
questions.explain: In the kidney, ADH receptors are coupled to adenylate cyclase (cAMP signaling), increasing surface expression of aquaporin water channels and promoting water retention (antidiuresis). In vascular smooth muscle, ADH causes direct vasoconstriction, raising blood pressure — hence the name 'vasopressin.' Option D incorrectly states ADH blocks aquaporins and causes diuresis, which is the opposite. The cGMP pathway (option E) is characteristic of ANP, not ADH.

questions.q: Prolactin and oxytocin both relate to lactation, but they differ in origin, type, and specific function. Which statement correctly distinguishes the two hormones?
options: Prolactin is a neurohormone from the posterior pituitary that stimulates milk ejection; oxytocin is a glandular hormone from the anterior pituitary that stimulates milk synthesis
Both prolactin and oxytocin are synthesized in hypothalamic SON and PVN neurons and released from the posterior pituitary
Prolactin (glandular hormone, anterior pituitary) stimulates milk synthesis; oxytocin (neurohormone, posterior pituitary) stimulates milk ejection
Prolactin stimulates uterine contractions during parturition; oxytocin stimulates milk protein synthesis in mammary acini
Both prolactin and oxytocin are tropic hormones that act on other endocrine glands to regulate lactation
questions.explain: Prolactin is a glandular hormone secreted by the anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis) that stimulates milk protein synthesis and mammary gland growth. Oxytocin is a neurohormone synthesized in hypothalamic magnocellular neurons (SON/PVN), stored in the posterior pituitary, and released to stimulate milk ejection (the milk ejection reflex) and uterine contractions. Options A and B have origins and functions reversed or incorrectly grouped.

questions.q: ADH and oxytocin are both 9-amino-acid nonapeptides with a disulfide bond. At which positions in the amino acid sequence do they differ?
options: Positions 1 and 9 — the terminal amino acids are different
Positions 2 and 7 — the tyrosine and proline positions are swapped
Positions 3 and 8 — phenylalanine (ADH) vs. isoleucine at position 3; arginine (ADH) vs. leucine at position 8
Positions 4 and 6 — the glutamine and cysteine positions
Positions 5 and 9 — the asparagine and glycine positions
questions.explain: ADH (argipressin) sequence: Cys-Tyr-Phe-Gln-Asn-Cys-Pro-Arg-Gly. Oxytocin: Cys-Tyr-Ile-Gln-Asn-Cys-Pro-Leu-Gly. Position 3 differs (Phe in ADH vs. Ile in oxytocin) and position 8 differs (Arg in ADH vs. Leu in oxytocin). Positions 1 and 2 (Cys and Tyr) and 4–7 (Gln-Asn-Cys-Pro) and 9 (Gly) are identical in both. The disulfide bond connects the cysteines at positions 1 and 6.

questions.q: Oxytocin has been called 'the hormone of love and bonding.' Which of the following correctly describes oxytocin's physiological roles beyond milk ejection?
options: Oxytocin inhibits uterine contractions during labor and promotes mammary gland synthesis of milk proteins
Oxytocin promotes social recognition, bonding, and trust in the brain as a neurotransmitter, and stimulates uterine contractions during and after parturition
Oxytocin acts as a tropic hormone on the ovaries to stimulate estrogen secretion during pregnancy
Oxytocin is released exclusively in females and has no role in male physiology
Oxytocin is a direct-acting anterior pituitary hormone released during suckling to stimulate milk synthesis
questions.explain: Beyond milk ejection, oxytocin: (1) stimulates uterine contractions during and after parturition; (2) acts as a neurotransmitter in the brain, where it is involved in social recognition, bonding, trust, and generosity, and is released during hugging, touching, and orgasm in BOTH sexes. Oxytocin is a neurohormone from the posterior pituitary (not an anterior pituitary direct-acting hormone), and it does not act on the ovaries as a tropic hormone.

questions.q: Which of the following is the correct sequence of events by which the hypothalamus controls cortisol secretion, starting from the hypothalamus and ending at the adrenal cortex?
options: Cortisol (adrenal cortex) → negative feedback to hypothalamus → CRH → anterior pituitary → ACTH (this is a feedback loop, not the stimulus pathway)
ACTH (released by hypothalamus) → travels in portal blood → reaches anterior pituitary → triggers CRH secretion → CRH stimulates adrenal cortex
CRH (from magnocellular parvocellular neurons, posterior pituitary) → systemic blood → adrenal cortex → ACTH → cortisol
CRH (from parvocellular neurons) → hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal system → anterior pituitary → ACTH released → adrenal cortex → cortisol
TRH (from parvocellular neurons) → portal system → anterior pituitary → ACTH → adrenal cortex → cortisol
questions.explain: Parvocellular neurons in the hypothalamus release CRH (corticotropin-releasing hormone) into the hypophyseal portal system. CRH travels via portal blood to the anterior pituitary, stimulating ACTH secretion. ACTH enters systemic circulation and stimulates the adrenal cortex to produce cortisol. Magnocellular neurons project to the posterior pituitary (not the portal system). TRH stimulates TSH (not ACTH). Option A describes the negative feedback loop, not the stimulus cascade.

lectures.title: Lecture 2: Endo3_4: Metabolic & Developmental Hormones + Water/Electrolyte Balance

questions.q: Which layer of the adrenal gland secretes catecholamines, and what is its embryological origin?
options: Adrenal medulla; ectodermal (neural)
Adrenal cortex; mesodermal (non-neural)
Adrenal medulla; mesodermal (non-neural)
Adrenal cortex; ectodermal (neural)
Both layers equally; mixed ectodermal and mesodermal
questions.explain: The adrenal medulla is the inner layer of the adrenal gland, is neural (ectodermal) in origin, and is the source of catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine). The adrenal cortex is the outer layer, is non-neural (mesodermal), and secretes mineralocorticoids, glucocorticoids, and androgens — not catecholamines. Confusing medulla with cortex is a classic exam error.

questions.q: Epinephrine is synthesized from norepinephrine by the enzyme PNMT. Which condition is required for this conversion step to occur?
options: Activation of preganglionic sympathetic fibers releasing acetylcholine
Cortisol stimulation from the adrenal cortex
High blood glucose stimulating chromaffin cells
Dopamine β-hydroxylase activity in the adrenal medulla
TSH stimulation from the anterior pituitary
questions.explain: PNMT (phenylethanolamine-N-methyltransferase) converts norepinephrine to epinephrine, and this step specifically requires cortisol from the adrenal cortex. Preganglionic acetylcholine activates chromaffin cells in general but does not specifically enable the PNMT-catalyzed conversion. Dopamine β-hydroxylase converts dopamine to norepinephrine — an earlier step in the pathway.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT an effect of epinephrine?
options: Increased insulin secretion
Increased glycogenolysis in the liver
Increased lipolysis in adipose tissue
Increased glucagon secretion
Vasodilation in skeletal muscle
questions.explain: Epinephrine actually DECREASES insulin secretion — a key metabolic effect that helps mobilize glucose during the fight-or-flight response. All other options are true effects: glycogenolysis and lipolysis provide fuel, glucagon secretion is increased, and vasodilation in skeletal muscle ensures blood flow to working muscles. This is a direct lecture quiz question.

questions.q: Secretion of catecholamines from the adrenal medulla is regulated by which of the following?
options: Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) from the anterior pituitary
Chromaffinotropic hormone from the hypothalamus
Growth hormone from the anterior pituitary
Insulin from the pancreatic beta cells
Neural input from preganglionic sympathetic fibers only
questions.explain: Catecholamine secretion from the adrenal medulla is regulated exclusively by neural input — preganglionic sympathetic fibers releasing acetylcholine activate chromaffin cells. There is no specific tropic hormone dedicated to controlling catecholamine release. The lecture explicitly states the answer is 'none of the above' when listing hormones, meaning neural control only.

questions.q: A patient presents with periodic hypertension, palpitations, sweating, and headaches. Imaging reveals a tumor of the adrenal medulla. Which hormone is most likely overproduced?
options: Cortisol
Aldosterone
Epinephrine
TSH
Glucagon
questions.explain: This describes pheochromocytoma, a tumor of the adrenal medulla that causes excess catecholamine (primarily epinephrine) secretion. The symptoms — periodic hypertension, palpitations, sweating, headaches, anxiety — result from excessive adrenergic stimulation. Cortisol excess causes Cushing's syndrome; aldosterone excess causes Conn's syndrome. Adrenal medulla tumors do not produce cortisol.

questions.q: Which adrenoreceptor subtype signals through adenylate cyclase inhibition to decrease intracellular cAMP?
options: α₂ receptor — inhibits adenylate cyclase, decreasing cAMP
α₁ receptor — signals via phospholipase C
β₁ receptor — signals via adenylate cyclase activation
β₂ receptor — signals via adenylate cyclase activation
All β receptors use the same inhibitory pathway
questions.explain: The α₂ receptor inhibits adenylate cyclase, decreasing cAMP. In contrast, α₁ receptors signal via phospholipase C → IP₃/DAG pathway, and β₁/β₂ receptors activate adenylate cyclase to increase cAMP. Clonidine is a clinical α₂ agonist. The other receptor subtypes listed all use different, non-inhibitory mechanisms.

questions.q: Epinephrine is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. Which combination of effects makes it effective?
options: Vasodilation and immunosuppression to reduce the allergic cascade
Histamine antagonism and mast cell stabilization at mucosal surfaces
Parasympathetic activation to counteract the hypotensive response
Vasoconstriction, bronchodilation, and cardiac inotropic/chronotropic effects
Increased insulin secretion to stabilize blood glucose during shock
questions.explain: In anaphylaxis, epinephrine prevents laryngeal edema and hypotension via vasoconstriction, opens airways via bronchodilation, and supports the heart via inotropic (increased force) and chronotropic (increased rate) effects. It does not antagonize histamine directly; that is the role of antihistamines. Epinephrine decreases insulin secretion, not increases it.

questions.q: Cortisol (hydrocortisone) was used with Ribavirin to treat SARS patients. Which action of cortisol provides the therapeutic rationale?
options: Increasing gluconeogenesis to restore blood glucose depleted by viral infection
Stimulating glycogen synthesis to sequester glucose from inflammatory cells
Promoting amino acid uptake in the liver to synthesize antiviral proteins
Suppressing the overactive immune response to prevent immune-mediated lung damage
Increasing lipolysis to provide energy substrates for activated immune cells
questions.explain: In SARS, much of the lung damage is caused by an overactive immune response (cytokine storm). Cortisol (hydrocortisone) suppresses inflammation and the immune system, reducing immune-mediated lung damage. Glucocorticoids are used as immunosuppressants after organ transplantation for the same reason. Increasing blood glucose or gluconeogenesis are effects of cortisol but are not the therapeutic rationale here.

questions.q: The HPA axis regulates glucocorticoid secretion. Which correctly describes the order of this axis?
options: ACTH (hypothalamus) → CRH (pituitary) → cortisol (adrenal cortex)
TRH (hypothalamus) → TSH (anterior pituitary) → cortisol (adrenal cortex)
CRH (hypothalamus) → ACTH (posterior pituitary) → cortisol (adrenal medulla)
GRH (hypothalamus) → GH (anterior pituitary) → cortisol (adrenal cortex)
CRH (hypothalamus) → ACTH (anterior pituitary) → cortisol (adrenal cortex)
questions.explain: The correct HPA axis sequence is: CRH from hypothalamic neurosecretory cells → ACTH from the anterior pituitary → cortisol secretion from the adrenal cortex. TRH/TSH controls thyroid hormones; GRH/GH is the growth hormone axis. Cortisol comes from the cortex not the medulla, and ACTH is from the anterior (not posterior) pituitary.

questions.q: Which set of metabolic effects best describes the primary actions of glucocorticoids (cortisol) during stress?
options: Increased glycogen synthesis in liver; increased glucose uptake in muscle and adipose
Decreased gluconeogenesis in liver; increased protein synthesis in muscle; increased lipogenesis in adipose
Increased gluconeogenesis in liver; decreased protein synthesis and glucose uptake in muscle; lipid mobilization in adipose
Increased glucose uptake in all tissues; decreased lipolysis in adipose
Stimulation of insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells to counteract stress hyperglycemia
questions.explain: Cortisol's primary effect is to promote gluconeogenesis in the liver, forming glucose from fats and proteins during stress. In skeletal muscle it decreases protein synthesis, increases protein degradation (supplying amino acids for gluconeogenesis), and reduces glucose uptake. In adipose tissue it decreases glucose uptake and increases lipid mobilization. This provides energy to critical tissues like the brain during starvation.

questions.q: A patient exhibits rapid weight gain, muscle weakness, skin thinning, immune suppression, and mood changes with elevated plasma cortisol. Which condition does this describe?
options: Addison's disease — adrenal cortex insufficiency
Conn's syndrome — excess aldosterone
Acromegaly — excess growth hormone in adulthood
Pheochromocytoma — adrenal medulla tumor
Cushing's syndrome — excess glucocorticoids
questions.explain: Cushing's syndrome is caused by excessive cortisol, whether from exogenous glucocorticoid drugs or from tumors producing cortisol, ACTH, or CRH. Hallmark symptoms include rapid weight gain, muscle weakness, skin thinning, immune suppression, and mood changes/depression. Addison's disease is adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol); Conn's is excess aldosterone; acromegaly involves GH excess.

questions.q: Which of the following tissues is EXCLUDED from thyroid hormone stimulation of cellular respiration?
options: Liver
Skeletal muscle
Adipose tissue
Cardiac muscle
Spleen
questions.explain: Thyroid hormones stimulate cellular respiration (basal metabolic rate) in almost all tissues, but the brain, spleen, testes/uterus, and the thyroid gland itself are explicitly excluded. Spleen is the correct excluded tissue among these options. Liver, skeletal muscle, adipose tissue, and cardiac muscle are all responsive to thyroid hormone metabolic stimulation.

questions.q: T4 is the major form of thyroid hormone released, but T3 is more potent. Which statement correctly describes the relationship between them?
options: T3 is secreted directly by the thyroid; T4 is produced by peripheral tissue deiodination
T4 is synthesized from one iodinated tyrosine; T3 from two iodinated tyrosines
T4 is the major form released by the thyroid and is converted to T3 in target tissues
Both T3 and T4 are released in equal amounts; potency is determined by receptor affinity only
T4 contains 3 iodine atoms; T3 contains 4 iodine atoms
questions.explain: T4 (tetraiodothyronine, 4 iodines) is the major form released by the thyroid gland. In target tissues, T4 is converted to the more potent T3 (triiodothyronine, 3 iodines). T4 has 4 iodines and T3 has 3 — the naming reflects iodine count directly. Both are formed from two iodinated tyrosine precursors joined together.

questions.q: A patient with hyperthyroidism shows increased heart rate and stronger cardiac contractions. Which thyroid hormone action most directly explains this?
options: Stimulating secretion of catecholamines from the adrenal medulla
Inhibiting the parasympathetic nervous system's effect on the heart
Directly stimulating cardiac beta-adrenergic receptors without epinephrine
Sensitizing cardiac tissues to epinephrine
Stimulating cellular respiration specifically in cardiac muscle
questions.explain: Thyroid hormones sensitize tissues to epinephrine. In hyperthyroidism, elevated thyroid hormone levels amplify the cardiac response to normal circulating epinephrine levels, causing increased heart rate and contractility. Thyroid hormones do not stimulate catecholamine secretion from the adrenal medulla — that is a distinct process. This is a specific lecture quiz distinction.

questions.q: Iodine deficiency leads to goiter (thyroid enlargement). What is the hormonal mechanism?
options: Low iodine → low T3/T4 → reduced negative feedback → high TSH → thyroid gland growth
Low iodine → low T3/T4 → reduced negative feedback → high CRH → thyroid hypertrophy
Low iodine → high T4 conversion to T3 → excessive TSH suppression → compensatory atrophy reversal
Low iodine → high TRH only → direct stimulation of thyroid cell division
Low iodine → low TSH → compensatory hypertrophy of thyroid follicular cells
questions.explain: Iodine deficiency prevents adequate T3/T4 synthesis. Low T3/T4 means reduced negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary, so TSH levels rise markedly. TSH not only stimulates thyroid hormone secretion but also thyroid gland growth — leading to goiter. CRH is part of the glucocorticoid axis, not the thyroid axis, and low iodine does not raise TSH by reducing TSH.

questions.q: Insulin and glucagon are secreted by different cells in the islets of Langerhans. Which statement about their structure is correct?
options: Both insulin and glucagon are steroid hormones acting via nuclear receptors
Insulin is a single-chain peptide of 29 amino acids; glucagon has 2 chains of 51 amino acids
Insulin is synthesized in alpha cells; glucagon is synthesized in beta cells
Both are single-chain peptides of identical length and opposite charge
Insulin is a 2-chain peptide of 51 amino acids; glucagon is a single-chain peptide of 29 amino acids
questions.explain: Insulin (from beta cells) consists of 2 chains with 51 amino acids total. Glucagon (from alpha cells) is a single-chain peptide of 29 amino acids. Both are peptide hormones — not steroids. The cell type assignments are frequently swapped in distractors: beta cells produce insulin, alpha cells produce glucagon.

questions.q: Which nutrient molecule has OPPOSITE effects on insulin secretion versus glucagon secretion?
options: Amino acids — stimulate both insulin and glucagon equally
Fatty acids — stimulate insulin but inhibit glucagon
Blood glucose — stimulates insulin and stimulates glucagon equally
Fatty acids — inhibit insulin but stimulate glucagon
Amino acids — stimulate glucagon but inhibit insulin
questions.explain: Fatty acids stimulate insulin secretion but inhibit glucagon secretion — opposite effects on the two hormones. Amino acids stimulate BOTH insulin and glucagon (same direction). Blood glucose stimulates insulin and inhibits glucagon — also opposite, but the comparison table in the lecture specifically highlights fatty acids as the classic differentiator between the two hormones' responses.

questions.q: A patient with Type 1 diabetes has absent insulin secretion. Which mechanism best explains development of ketoacidosis?
options: Low insulin → low glucose uptake → cells burn amino acids excessively → ammonia toxicity
Low insulin → reduced gluconeogenesis → brain starvation → lactic acidosis buildup
Low insulin → high glucagon (no inhibition) → excess ketone formation by liver → ketoacidosis
Low insulin → increased fatty acid storage → reduced beta-oxidation → organic acid buildup
Low insulin → reduced glycogenolysis → severe energy deficit → metabolic acidosis
questions.explain: In Type 1 diabetes, absent insulin removes its inhibitory effect on glucagon, so glucagon levels become very high. Excess glucagon drives ketone body formation by the liver, leading to ketoacidosis, electrolyte imbalance, dehydration, and potentially coma or death. Lactic acidosis and ammonia toxicity are not the primary mechanisms of diabetic ketoacidosis.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT usually associated with Type 1 diabetes mellitus?
options: High blood glucose (hyperglycemia)
Excessive urination and thirst
Ketoacidosis from elevated glucagon
Low glucagon level in blood
Retinopathy (damaged retinal vessels)
questions.explain: In Type 1 diabetes, glucagon levels are HIGH (not low), because insulin normally suppresses glucagon secretion and that suppression is absent. Hyperglycemia, excessive urination and thirst, ketoacidosis from high glucagon, and retinopathy (a chronic vascular complication) are all typically associated with Type 1 diabetes. Low glucagon is the one that does NOT belong.

questions.q: GH promotes linear skeletal growth primarily through which mechanism?
options: GH promotes amino acid uptake in muscle; tension from enlarged muscle secondarily stimulates bone
GH acts on the liver to produce IGF-II, which specifically promotes linear bone growth
GH directly stimulates chondrocytes in growth plates via its anti-insulin effects
Direct action of GH on osteoblasts in bone to stimulate bone matrix deposition
GH acts on the liver to produce IGF-I, which then promotes linear growth of the skeleton
questions.explain: Most growth-promoting effects of GH are indirect, mediated by IGFs (somatomedins) produced by the liver. IGF-I specifically promotes linear growth of the skeleton; IGF-II promotes growth of many other tissues and organs. GH's direct action on osteoblasts is not the primary mechanism for linear growth. IGF-II is not the skeletal-specific factor — that is IGF-I.

questions.q: Which of the following is a STIMULUS for growth hormone secretion?
options: High blood glucose (hyperglycemia)
Arginine (amino acid)
REM sleep (dreaming stage)
GIH (somatostatin) from the hypothalamus
High IGF levels feeding back on the pituitary
questions.explain: Arginine is a specific stimulus for GH secretion and is used clinically in GH stimulation tests. Hyperglycemia inhibits GH; hypoglycemia stimulates it. REM (dreaming) sleep inhibits GH, while deep non-REM sleep stimulates it. Somatostatin (GIH) inhibits GH from the hypothalamus. High IGF levels provide negative feedback, inhibiting GH release.

questions.q: GH deficiency in a child versus in an adult leads to different outcomes. Which comparison is correct?
options: Child: acromegaly; Adult: gigantism — bone response reverses with age
Child: dwarfism; Adult: no evident effect — bone growth is already complete in adults
Child: no evident effect; Adult: dwarfism — adults are more sensitive to GH loss
Child: dwarfism; Adult: acromegaly — GH deficiency causes paradoxical bone thickening
Child: gigantism; Adult: no evident effect — excess GH causes growth in children only
questions.explain: GH deficiency during childhood (when bone growth plates are open) causes dwarfism. GH deficiency in adults (after normal bone growth is complete) has no evident effect. Gigantism and acromegaly result from GH EXCESS, not deficiency. In adults, excess GH causes acromegaly (enlarged extremities, not increased height) because bone plates are fused.

questions.q: A doctor proposes injecting insulin into a patient to test whether the patient's pituitary can secrete growth hormone. What is the physiological rationale?
options: Insulin directly stimulates the anterior pituitary to release GH via cAMP signaling
Insulin → hyperglycemia → stimulates GH release as a metabolic counter-measure
Insulin → hypoglycemia → stimulates GH release as a counter-regulatory response
Insulin competes with somatostatin for hypothalamic receptors, releasing GH inhibition
Insulin raises plasma amino acid levels, which are a strong GH stimulus
questions.explain: Injecting insulin causes hypoglycemia (low blood glucose), which is a strong stimulus for GH secretion. If the pituitary is functioning normally, GH levels should rise after the insulin-induced hypoglycemia. This is a legitimate clinical test of GH secretory capacity. Insulin does not directly stimulate the pituitary, cause hyperglycemia, or compete with somatostatin.

questions.q: Leptin is encoded by the obese (ob) gene. Which receptor type does it signal through, and where is this receptor primarily expressed?
options: GPCR linked to cAMP signaling; primarily expressed in adipose tissue
Nuclear steroid receptor acting as transcription factor; expressed in the hypothalamus
Receptor tyrosine kinase with intrinsic kinase activity; expressed in peripheral organs
Cytokine family receptor (JAK-associated); primarily expressed in the brain
Ion-channel-linked receptor; expressed in hypothalamic arcuate nucleus neurons
questions.explain: The leptin receptor (LepR/OB-R) is a cytokine family receptor that is associated with JAK tyrosine kinases (it does not have intrinsic kinase activity). It is expressed primarily in the brain, and most of leptin's effects on end organs are indirect via the CNS. Leptin is a peptide hormone, so it does not use nuclear steroid receptors. The insulin receptor is the receptor tyrosine kinase with intrinsic activity.

questions.q: Obese individuals paradoxically have HIGH leptin levels despite leptin normally reducing appetite. Which is the best explanation?
options: Obesity causes downregulation of the ob gene, so less leptin is produced per fat cell
Obese individuals produce no leptin because their adipose tissue becomes dysfunctional
Obese individuals have low cortisol, which normally suppresses leptin production
High body fat suppresses leptin receptor expression specifically in peripheral tissues
Partial leptin resistance at the site of action means more leptin is needed for the same effect
questions.explain: High leptin in obesity is explained by: (1) greater percentage body fat producing more leptin overall, (2) partial leptin resistance at the brain receptor site requiring higher concentrations for the same inhibitory effect on appetite, and (3) obese individuals being in a state of perceived starvation. The ob gene is not downregulated in obesity. Leptin resistance in the brain is the key mechanism explaining elevated leptin alongside obesity.

questions.q: ADH increases water reabsorption in the kidney through which molecular mechanism?
options: Activating Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase to increase sodium-driven osmotic water reabsorption
Increasing surface expression of aquaporin water channels in kidney tubule cells
Stimulating aldosterone receptors on kidney collecting duct cells to retain water
Blocking K⁺ excretion channels, creating an osmotic gradient for water retention
Activating angiotensin-converting enzyme to produce angiotensin II in the kidney
questions.explain: ADH increases water reabsorption by increasing the surface expression of aquaporin water channel proteins in kidney tubule cells. This allows water to move osmotically from the tubule lumen into the blood. ADH does not activate aldosterone receptors — aldosterone works on sodium channels independently. ADH is distinct from the RAAS pathway involving angiotensin-converting enzyme.

questions.q: Which of the following will NOT stimulate ADH secretion?
options: Eating salty food (raises plasma osmolality)
Hemorrhage exceeding 10% of blood volume
Increased plasma osmolality above 280 mOsm
Drinking a large volume of water
Stretch receptor signals from the cardiovascular system due to low blood pressure
questions.explain: Drinking a large volume of water DECREASES plasma osmolality, which INHIBITS ADH secretion (reducing water reabsorption to restore normal osmolality). Salty food increases osmolality → stimulates ADH. Hemorrhage → decreased blood volume/pressure → stretch receptors signal the pituitary → ADH rises. Increased plasma osmolality also stimulates ADH via osmoreceptors in the anterior hypothalamus.

questions.q: The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) is activated when blood pressure falls. Which is the correct sequence of events?
options: Low BP → juxtaglomerular apparatus releases renin → angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → ACE (lung/kidney) → angiotensin II → aldosterone secretion
Low BP → adrenal cortex releases renin → angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → ACE (liver) → angiotensin II → aldosterone
Low BP → posterior pituitary releases ADH → angiotensin II → aldosterone from adrenal medulla
Low BP → anterior pituitary releases ACTH → angiotensin I → ACE → angiotensin II → aldosterone
Low BP → hypothalamus releases CRH → angiotensin II → aldosterone from adrenal cortex
questions.explain: In the RAAS: decreased renal perfusion → juxtaglomerular apparatus releases renin → renin cleaves angiotensinogen to angiotensin I → ACE in lung/kidney endothelium converts angiotensin I to angiotensin II → angiotensin II stimulates aldosterone from the adrenal cortex, causes vasoconstriction, stimulates ADH, and increases sympathetic activity. Renin comes from the kidney (juxtaglomerular apparatus), not the adrenal cortex.

questions.q: Atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) is secreted in response to which stimulus, and what is its primary effect?
options: Low blood volume → decreases sodium excretion to conserve fluid volume
High plasma osmolality → increases water reabsorption in kidney tubules
High plasma K⁺ → stimulates Na⁺ reabsorption similar to aldosterone
Low blood pressure → vasoconstriction and water retention to restore perfusion
Mechanical stretch of cardiac atria due to high blood volume → increases Na⁺ excretion, opposes ADH/aldosterone
questions.explain: ANP is secreted by endocrine cells in the cardiac atria in response to mechanical stretch, which signals high blood volume. It acts on the kidneys to increase Na⁺ excretion (natriuresis) and urine production, thereby reducing blood volume and pressure. ANP OPPOSES the effects of ADH and aldosterone — it does not promote water or sodium retention. It lowers blood pressure, not increases it.

questions.q: ADH, aldosterone, and ANP all influence kidney function and blood pressure. Which statement correctly compares their actions?
options: All three increase blood pressure via vasoconstriction or increased blood volume
All three increase Na⁺ reabsorption in the kidney collecting duct
All three are peptide hormones and share the same second messenger pathway
All three act on the kidney, but their net effects on Na⁺ and blood pressure are not uniform — none of the above statements is true
All three are regulated primarily by changes in plasma osmolality
questions.explain: ADH and aldosterone increase blood pressure, while ANP decreases it — so they do not all raise BP. ADH promotes water retention (indirect Na⁺ effect), aldosterone promotes Na⁺ reabsorption, ANP promotes Na⁺ excretion — they do not all increase Na⁺ reabsorption. Aldosterone is a steroid hormone, not a peptide — so not all three are peptides. The lecture's quiz answer is 'none of the above' because no single listed property applies uniformly to all three.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the biosynthetic pathway of steroid reproductive hormones?
options: Cholesterol → androgens → progesterone → estrogens
Cholesterol → estrogens → progesterone → androgens
Cholesterol → progesterone → androgens → estrogens
Progesterone → cholesterol → androgens → estrogens
Cholesterol → androgens → estrogens → progesterone
questions.explain: The lecture explicitly states the biosynthetic sequence: cholesterol → progesterone → androgens → estrogens. Options A and E place progesterone after androgens, reversing the order. This pathway is the foundation for understanding why estrogens are the final product and require prior androgen intermediates.

questions.q: Which of the following is TRUE regarding reproductive hormones?
options: All reproductive hormones are steroids.
All steroids are reproductive hormones.
All reproductive hormones are secreted from sex organs.
Androgens are present only in males.
None of the above.
questions.explain: None of statements 1–4 is true. Oxytocin and prolactin are peptide reproductive hormones, not steroids. Many steroids (e.g. cortisol) are not reproductive. Oxytocin and prolactin are secreted from the pituitary, not sex organs. Androgens are also produced by the female adrenal cortex. This is a verbatim in-lecture quiz question.

questions.q: Leydig cells are BEST characterised as cells that:
options: Line the interior of seminiferous tubules and form the blood-testes barrier
Synthesise inhibin in response to FSH stimulation
Produce testosterone in response to LH stimulation
Nourish developing germ cells by producing seminiferous tubular fluid
Differentiate into mature sperm after a series of divisions
questions.explain: Leydig (interstitial) cells are located outside the seminiferous tubules and produce testosterone in response to LH. Options A, B, and D describe Sertoli cell functions. Option E describes germ cells. The Leydig/Sertoli distinction is a frequent exam point.

questions.q: Which of the following concerning Leydig cells is FALSE?
options: They secrete androgens.
They are the target of LH.
They are located outside the seminiferous tubules.
They secrete inhibin.
They are also called interstitial cells.
questions.explain: Inhibin is secreted by Sertoli cells in males, not by Leydig cells. Leydig cells do secrete androgens (testosterone), respond to LH, lie outside the seminiferous tubules, and are called interstitial cells — all of these are true. This is a direct in-lecture quiz question.

questions.q: Sertoli cells perform all of the following functions EXCEPT:
options: Binding LH and transducing its signal to Leydig cells
Contributing to seminiferous tubular fluid production
Nourishing developing sperm
Secreting inhibin
Forming the blood-testes barrier
questions.explain: Sertoli cells respond to FSH, not LH; LH acts directly on Leydig cells. Sertoli cells do produce seminiferous tubular fluid, nourish developing sperm, secrete inhibin, and form the blood-testes barrier. This is a direct in-lecture quiz question.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the role of inhibin in the male?
options: It inhibits both LH and FSH release from the anterior pituitary.
It inhibits GnRH release from the hypothalamus.
It selectively inhibits FSH release from the anterior pituitary.
It stimulates Leydig cells to produce testosterone.
It promotes differentiation of spermatogonia into spermatozoa.
questions.explain: Inhibin, a peptide hormone from Sertoli cells, specifically provides negative feedback to inhibit FSH release from the anterior pituitary only. Testosterone handles negative feedback on GnRH, LH, and FSH. Inhibin does not act on Leydig cells or directly on spermatogenesis.

questions.q: Both LH and FSH are required for spermatogenesis. Which pairing best summarises their respective primary targets and direct outcomes?
options: LH → Sertoli cells → inhibin; FSH → Leydig cells → testosterone
LH → Leydig cells → testosterone; FSH → Sertoli cells → spermatogenesis support
LH → seminiferous tubules → sperm maturation; FSH → Leydig cells → androgen synthesis
LH → hypothalamus → GnRH; FSH → Sertoli cells → inhibin
LH → Sertoli cells → spermatogenesis; FSH → Leydig cells → testosterone
questions.explain: LH targets Leydig cells to drive testosterone production. FSH targets Sertoli cells to support spermatogenesis (which also requires testosterone). All other options swap the targets or assign incorrect outcomes.

questions.q: Prolonged aerobic exercise inhibits GnRH secretion. What is the DIRECT consequence in a male?
options: Increased testosterone due to direct Leydig cell stimulation
Increased inhibin secretion from Sertoli cells
Decreased FSH and LH, leading to inhibition of sperm production
Increased LH surge, triggering release of stored sperm
Decreased inhibin, causing compensatory rise in FSH
questions.explain: Suppressed GnRH means less stimulation of the anterior pituitary, so FSH and LH fall. With less LH, Leydig cell testosterone production drops; with less FSH and testosterone, spermatogenesis is inhibited. Exercise does not directly stimulate Leydig cells or cause LH surges.

questions.q: Testosterone:
options: Is secreted only by Sertoli cells.
Is secreted only by Leydig cells.
Acts only on Sertoli cells.
Promotes protein degradation.
None of the above.
questions.explain: None of the first four options is correct. Testosterone is produced by Leydig cells AND the adrenal cortex. It acts on many target tissues (seminiferous tubules, somatic tissues, brain, primary and secondary sex organs), not only Sertoli cells. It promotes protein synthesis, not degradation. This is a direct in-lecture quiz question.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a recognised action of testosterone?
options: Triggering primary male sex characteristics in prenatal development
Stimulating spermatogenesis
Promoting closure of epiphyseal plates in long bones
Increasing muscle mass through protein synthesis
Causing behavioural changes such as increased aggression
questions.explain: Epiphyseal (骨骺) closure from sex steroid exposure is primarily an effect of estrogen, not testosterone. The lecture attributes early epiphyseal closure to estrogen. All other options — prenatal primary sex characteristic development, spermatogenesis, muscle protein synthesis, and aggression — are explicitly listed as testosterone actions.

questions.q: The scrotum houses the testes externally because:
options: It provides easier access for sperm transport through the vas deferens.
Spermatogenesis requires a temperature lower than normal body temperature.
Leydig cells require lower blood pressure to produce testosterone.
The epididymis requires warming to complete sperm maturation.
External placement allows direct hormonal sensing of ambient temperature.
questions.explain: The lecture explicitly states that the scrotum houses the testes outside the abdominal cavity because sperm production does not occur at normal body temperature. Lower scrotal temperature (~2°C below core) is required for spermatogenesis. Temperature plays no role in Leydig cell testosterone synthesis or epididymal maturation in this context.

questions.q: Which gland contributes approximately 60% of semen fluid volume and provides fructose as an energy source for sperm?
options: Prostate gland
Bulbourethral gland
Epididymis
Seminal vesicle
Adrenal cortex
questions.explain: The seminal vesicles add 60% of fluid to semen and contain fructose (energy source), prostaglandins, and coagulating proteins. The prostate gland neutralises acid. The bulbourethral gland also contributes fluid. The epididymis is where final sperm maturation occurs, not fluid production.

questions.q: What is the site of final sperm maturation in the male reproductive tract?
options: Seminiferous tubules
Vas deferens
Prostate gland
Epididymis
Urethra
questions.explain: The epididymis is the site of final sperm maturation, a process that takes several weeks. Spermatogenesis (production) occurs in the seminiferous tubules. The vas deferens transports sperm. The prostate and urethra do not perform maturation functions.

questions.q: In the female, fertilisation of the ovum normally occurs in which structure?
options: Uterus
Ovary
Endometrium
Cervix
Fallopian tube (oviduct)
questions.explain: The fallopian tube (uterine tube / oviduct) is the site of fertilisation; the fertilised ovum then travels to the uterus for implantation in the endometrium. The ovary produces ova but is not the site of fertilisation. This is a direct in-lecture quiz question (oviduct function).

questions.q: A woman's ovaries are best described as:
options: Secondary sex organs that transport ova to the uterus
Primary sex organs that produce ova and secrete estrogens and progesterone within follicles
Endocrine glands that secrete only estrogen and no progesterone
Structures whose hormonal production occurs primarily in the endometrium
Organs whose sole function is oogenesis with no endocrine role
questions.explain: The ovaries are the primary sex organs; they produce ova and secrete both estrogens and progesterone, with egg production and hormone production all occurring within the follicles. They are not secondary sex organs. Both estrogen and progesterone are produced (not estrogen alone). The endometrium does not produce reproductive hormones.

questions.q: After ovulation, what happens to the ruptured follicle?
options: It degenerates immediately and stops hormone secretion.
It transforms into the corpus luteum and secretes progesterone and some estrogen.
It becomes the primary follicle of the next cycle.
It migrates to the uterus and becomes the endometrium.
It is reabsorbed into the ovarian stroma without any hormonal activity.
questions.explain: After ovulation, the ruptured follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone and some estrogen. These hormones maintain the endometrium and exert negative feedback on the hypothalamus. The corpus luteum only degenerates if no fertilisation/implantation occurs.

questions.q: During the follicular phase of the ovarian cycle, rising estrogen levels provide which type of feedback to the hypothalamus, and what is the consequence?
options: Negative feedback, suppressing GnRH and preventing premature LH surge
Positive feedback, amplifying GnRH/LH/FSH release and ultimately triggering ovulation
No feedback; estrogen acts only on the uterus during this phase
Negative feedback on FSH only, while stimulating LH directly
Positive feedback on inhibin, which then stimulates FSH release
questions.explain: During the late follicular phase (days 10–12), increasing estrogen from the growing follicle provides positive feedback to the hypothalamus, increasing GnRH/LH/FSH release. This culminates in the LH/FSH surge around day 12–14, which triggers ovulation. Negative feedback is the mode in the luteal phase, not the follicular phase.

questions.q: Which of the following is FALSE concerning the female reproductive cycle?
options: LH secretion is inhibited by estrogen and progesterone in the luteal phase.
The corpus luteum secretes estradiol and progesterone.
FSH release is partly responsible for follicular maturation.
The corpus luteum does not regress if human chorionic gonadotropins are produced.
Ovulation is prevented by follicular estrogen secretion.
questions.explain: Statement 5 is false: follicular estrogen does NOT prevent ovulation — instead, high follicular estrogen triggers the LH surge that CAUSES ovulation. Ovulation is prevented during the luteal phase by high progesterone and estrogen combined. Statements 1–4 are all true and are directly from the lecture.

questions.q: Ovulation can be prevented by:
options: High levels of estrogen and progesterone together
FSH alone
LH alone
GnRH alone
None of the above
questions.explain: High combined levels of estrogen and progesterone (as in the luteal phase or during pregnancy) provide negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary, suppressing FSH and LH and preventing further ovulation. GnRH, FSH, and LH individually stimulate rather than prevent ovulation. This is a direct in-lecture quiz question.

questions.q: What is the role of chorionic gonadotropin (CG/hCG) in early pregnancy?
options: It stimulates the anterior pituitary to release more FSH for follicle development.
It triggers ovulation of a second egg after fertilisation.
It rescues the corpus luteum, preventing its degeneration and maintaining progesterone/estrogen production.
It directly stimulates endometrial growth by binding estrogen receptors.
It replaces progesterone production by the corpus luteum immediately after implantation.
questions.explain: Within a day of implantation, the placenta begins secreting hCG, which acts like LH to rescue the corpus luteum from degeneration. This allows the corpus luteum to continue producing progesterone and estrogen through the first trimester. hCG does not trigger FSH release, does not cause a second ovulation, and does not replace progesterone immediately.

questions.q: During the luteal phase, inhibin released from the follicle at ovulation specifically inhibits which pituitary hormone?
options: LH only
Both FSH and LH equally
FSH only
Prolactin only
Both GnRH and FSH
questions.explain: The lecture states that inhibin released from the follicle at ovulation inhibits FSH release specifically. In males, Sertoli cell-derived inhibin also selectively inhibits FSH. Testosterone and the combined estrogen/progesterone exert the broader negative feedback on LH and GnRH.

questions.q: A 24-week pregnant woman is found to have elevated blood glucose. Which mechanism, as described in the lecture, best explains this finding?
options: Excess progesterone stimulating hepatic gluconeogenesis
Placental estrogen decreasing peripheral tissue sensitivity to insulin
Oxytocin directly inhibiting insulin secretion from the pancreas
Increased carbohydrate absorption in the GI tract during pregnancy
Excessive secretion of glucagon from the pancreatic alpha cells
questions.explain: The lecture attributes gestational diabetes to placental estrogen decreasing peripheral insulin sensitivity. As the placenta grows it produces more estrogen, worsening insulin resistance. Options A, C, D, and E are not stated in the lecture as mechanisms; gestational diabetes is specifically an estrogen-mediated phenomenon.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly pairs an effect with the hormone responsible, according to the lecture?
options: Bone protection against loss in women — progesterone
Stimulation of mammary gland development — estrogen only
Prevention of further ovulation during pregnancy — progesterone
Antagonism of dihydrotestosterone effects — estrogen
Early closure of epiphyseal plates — testosterone
questions.explain: Progesterone provides negative feedback to the hypothalamus to prevent further ovulation during pregnancy. Bone protection against loss is attributed to estrogen, not progesterone. Mammary gland development involves both. Antagonism of DHT effects is attributed to progesterone. Epiphyseal closure is driven by estrogen, not testosterone.

questions.q: The contraceptive pill prevents pregnancy by which TWO primary mechanisms?
options: Blocking estrogen receptors in the ovary and thickening the fallopian tube wall
Suppressing gonadotropin release to prevent ovulation, and increasing cervical mucus viscosity to inhibit sperm mobility
Preventing implantation of the fertilised ovum in the endometrium and suppressing FSH only
Destroying the corpus luteum and blocking progesterone receptors in the uterus
Stimulating inhibin release and directly inactivating sperm in the vagina
questions.explain: The lecture states the pill works by suppressing gonadotropin release (preventing ovulation) and by increasing cervical mucus viscosity (inhibiting sperm mobility). It does not block estrogen receptors, destroy the corpus luteum, or act on sperm directly.

questions.q: For in vitro fertilisation (IVF), which hormones are used for ovarian hyperstimulation and for final oocyte maturation induction, respectively?
options: FSH for hyperstimulation; LH for final maturation
LH for hyperstimulation; FSH for final maturation
FSH for hyperstimulation; human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) for final maturation
GnRH for hyperstimulation; oxytocin for final maturation
Estrogen for hyperstimulation; progesterone for final maturation
questions.explain: The lecture specifies that IVF uses FSH for ovarian hyperstimulation to generate multiple eggs, and hCG for final maturation induction of oocytes. LH alone is not the maturation agent used in IVF. This reflects the clinical use of these hormones in the IVF protocol.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly distinguishes primary sex characteristics from secondary sex characteristics?
options: Primary sex characteristics develop at puberty; secondary sex characteristics are present at birth.
Primary sex characteristics are directly related to reproduction and include the genitalia; secondary sex characteristics distinguish the sexes but are not directly reproductive.
Primary sex characteristics include voice quality and breast development; secondary sex characteristics include the gonads.
Primary sex characteristics are regulated by peptide hormones; secondary sex characteristics are regulated by steroids.
There is no functional distinction; both categories include the genitalia.
questions.explain: Primary sex characteristics are directly related to reproduction and include the genitalia. Secondary sex characteristics (e.g. voice, facial hair, breast development) distinguish the sexes without being directly reproductive. Options A, C, D, and E misattribute or conflate the two categories.

questions.q: Which statement about progesterone is correct?
options: It stimulates gonadotropin release during the luteal phase.
It promotes ovulation by triggering an LH surge.
It antagonises the effects of testosterone and dihydrotestosterone in females.
It is produced exclusively by the corpus luteum.
It decreases sensitivity of peripheral tissues to insulin.
questions.explain: The lecture explicitly states that progesterone antagonises the effects of testosterone and dihydrotestosterone, preventing target tissues from responding to androgens in the female. Progesterone inhibits (not stimulates) gonadotropin release and suppresses ovulation during pregnancy. It is produced by both the corpus luteum and the adrenal cortex. Insulin sensitivity reduction is an effect of estrogen, not progesterone.

questions.q: A male athlete undergoes months of intensive training. He is found to have reduced sperm count. Which is the most likely hormonal explanation based on lecture content?
options: Excess testosterone from physical training directly suppresses spermatogenesis.
Prolonged exercise stress suppresses GnRH, reducing FSH and LH, impairing spermatogenesis.
Increased inhibin from Leydig cells suppresses FSH selectively, halting sperm production.
Exercise increases blood pressure, damaging seminiferous tubules directly.
Increased prolactin during exercise inhibits testosterone synthesis.
questions.explain: The lecture states that prolonged stress (e.g. protracted aerobic exercise) inhibits GnRH secretion, which reduces FSH and LH from the anterior pituitary, thereby impairing spermatogenesis. Option C incorrectly attributes inhibin to Leydig cells. Options D and E are not in the lecture.

questions.q: The endometrium undergoes which sequence of changes in the uterine cycle?
options: Proliferative → secretory → menses
Menses → secretory → proliferative
Secretory → menses → proliferative
Menses → proliferative → secretory
Proliferative → menses → secretory
questions.explain: The uterine cycle begins with menses (menstrual bleeding as the old endometrium is shed), followed by the proliferative phase (endometrium rebuilds under estrogen influence), and then the secretory phase (corpus luteum hormones enhance endometrium secretions in preparation for implantation). This aligns with the ovarian follicular → ovulation → luteal phases.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the gonads?
options: Producing gametes
Secreting sex hormones that regulate the reproductive process
Regulating development and function of secondary sex organs
Transporting and nourishing the embryo during development
Acting as endocrine glands that influence secondary sex characteristics
questions.explain: Transporting and nourishing the embryo is the function of secondary sex organs (such as the uterus and fallopian tubes), not the gonads. Gonads produce gametes, secrete sex hormones, and regulate the development and function of other reproductive organs.

lectures.title: Lecture 4: GI1: Overview of the Digestive System

questions.q: Which of the following is the best definition of the digestive system?
options: The alimentary canal alone, responsible for food breakdown
The alimentary canal and accessory digestive organs as an integrated system for ingestion, digestion, and absorption
