The stomach and intestines working together to absorb nutrients
The set of organs that produce digestive enzymes and hormones
The gastrointestinal tract plus the circulatory system
questions.explain: The digestive system is defined as the alimentary canal AND accessory digestive organs (e.g., liver, pancreas, salivary glands) working as an integrated system. The alimentary canal alone is not the complete digestive system, and the circulatory system is not part of it.

questions.q: Segmentation in the intestine is best classified as which type of digestion?
options: Chemical digestion, because it involves enzyme secretion
Mechanical digestion, because it physically mixes and breaks down food
Both mechanical and chemical digestion simultaneously
Neither; segmentation is a form of absorption
Chemical digestion, because it requires energy from ATP
questions.explain: Segmentation is rhythmic local constriction of the intestinal wall that physically mixes and breaks food particles — this is mechanical digestion. No enzymes are involved in the segmentation movement itself, so it is not chemical digestion.

questions.q: Which three monosaccharides are produced by carbohydrate digestion and absorbed into the bloodstream?
options: Glucose, sucrose, and galactose
Fructose, maltose, and glucose
Glucose, fructose, and galactose
Galactose, ribose, and glucose
Sucrose, fructose, and lactose
questions.explain: Carbohydrates are broken down to monosaccharides — specifically glucose, fructose, and galactose — which are then absorbed into the bloodstream. Sucrose, maltose, and lactose are disaccharides that must be further broken down; ribose is a pentose not produced from dietary polysaccharide digestion.

questions.q: Dietary fats are mainly which type of molecule, and what are their digestion end-products absorbed by the intestine?
options: Phospholipids; broken down into fatty acids and glycerophosphate
Cholesterol esters; broken down into free cholesterol and fatty acids
Triglycerides; broken down into glycerol and free fatty acids
Triglycerides; broken down into glucose and fatty acids
Sterols; broken down into bile acids and fatty acids
questions.explain: Dietary fat consists mainly of triglycerides. These are hydrolysed into glycerol and free fatty acids, which are then absorbed by the intestine. Phospholipids and cholesterol esters are not the primary dietary fat form, and glucose is not a product of fat digestion.

questions.q: What is the primary function of the stomach in the GI tract?
options: Absorption of all major food products into the blood
Final chemical digestion of all carbohydrates
Receiving, mechanically mixing food with secretions, and partial digestion — with no absorption
Secretion of bile to emulsify fats
Storage and concentration of undigested waste for defecation
questions.explain: The stomach acts as a reservoir, mechanically mixes food with saliva and gastric secretions, and performs only partial digestion. Critically, no absorption of food products occurs in the stomach. Bile is produced by the liver, and final absorption and waste storage are functions of other regions.

questions.q: Which region of the GI tract is primarily responsible for the absorption of ALL food products?
options: Stomach
Esophagus
Large intestine
Small intestine
Pharynx
questions.explain: Absorption of all food products (amino acids, monosaccharides, fatty acids, etc.) occurs in the small intestine (midgut). The stomach performs no significant absorption. The large intestine absorbs water, ions, and some vitamins but not the major food products.

questions.q: Bile is produced by which organ and stored by which organ?
options: Produced by the gallbladder; stored by the pancreas
Produced by the pancreas; stored by the liver
Produced by the liver; stored by the gallbladder
Produced by the gallbladder; stored by the liver
Produced and stored by the liver
questions.explain: Bile is produced (secreted) by the liver and stored and concentrated between meals by the gallbladder. The pancreas produces digestive enzymes and bicarbonate, not bile. Confusing the site of production with the site of storage is a common error.

questions.q: What exocrine secretions does the stomach produce, and what is the function of HCl specifically?
options: Amylase, lipase, and bicarbonate; HCl digests proteins
HCl, pepsin, and mucus; HCl solubilises food particles and kills microbes
Bile salts, bicarbonate, and pepsin; HCl neutralises alkaline food
HCl, amylase, and mucus; HCl activates amylase
Pepsin, lipase, and bicarbonate; HCl emulsifies fat
questions.explain: The stomach secretes HCl, pepsin (a protein-digesting enzyme), and mucus. HCl specifically solubilises food particles and kills microbes. Amylase is a salivary/pancreatic enzyme; bile is from the liver; bicarbonate comes from the pancreas and liver.

questions.q: Which organ secretes bicarbonate into the small intestine primarily to neutralise HCl arriving from the stomach?
options: Liver only
Gallbladder
Stomach
Pancreas only
Both the pancreas and liver secrete bicarbonate for this purpose
questions.explain: According to the lecture table, both the pancreas and the liver secrete bicarbonate to neutralise HCl entering the small intestine from the stomach. The gallbladder only stores bile; the stomach secretes HCl itself.

questions.q: What are the four microscopic layers of the GI tract wall, listed from innermost (lumen) to outermost?
options: Serosa, muscularis, submucosa, mucosa
Mucosa, muscularis, submucosa, serosa
Mucosa, submucosa, muscularis, serosa
Submucosa, mucosa, serosa, muscularis
Epithelium, lamina propria, submucosa, serosa
questions.explain: From the lumen outward, the four layers are: mucosa (innermost), submucosa, muscularis, and serosa (outermost). The muscularis comes after the submucosa, not before it. The epithelium and lamina propria are sub-components of the mucosa, not separate wall layers.

questions.q: Where is the myenteric plexus (plexus of Auerbach) located in the GI wall?
options: Within the mucosa, between the epithelium and lamina propria
Within the submucosa
Between the longitudinal and circular muscle layers of the muscularis
External to the serosa
Within the lamina propria of the mucosa
questions.explain: The myenteric plexus (Auerbach) lies between the longitudinal and circular muscle layers of the muscularis and primarily controls GI motility. The submucosal plexus (Meissner) is in the submucosa and primarily controls secretion. These two plexuses are frequently swapped.

questions.q: The submucosal plexus (plexus of Meissner) is located in which layer of the GI wall?
options: Between the two muscle layers of the muscularis
Within the mucosa
In the submucosa
In the serosa
Between the mucosa and submucosa only
questions.explain: The submucosal plexus (Meissner) is located within the submucosa and primarily controls glandular secretion and mucosal movement. The myenteric plexus (Auerbach) sits between the muscle layers and controls motility. Exam questions often test this distinction.

questions.q: Peristalsis differs from segmentation in that peristalsis:
options: Involves only circular muscle and does not produce net propulsion
Involves both longitudinal and circular muscles and primarily propels food aborally
Occurs exclusively in the large intestine
Is initiated by chemical stimulants in the lumen rather than distention
Involves only longitudinal muscle and is asynchronous
questions.explain: Peristalsis is a travelling wave of constriction caused by coordinated action of BOTH longitudinal and circular smooth muscles, moving food aborally (away from the mouth). Segmentation, by contrast, involves only circular muscle and is asynchronous — its primary function is mixing, not net propulsion.

questions.q: Which statement about segmentation is correct?
options: It involves both longitudinal and circular muscles contracting in sequence
Its primary function is to propel food aborally
It is a travelling wave that moves along the intestine
It involves asynchronous contractions of circular muscle only, with no longitudinal muscle
It occurs exclusively in the esophagus
questions.explain: Segmentation is an asynchronous, non-travelling constriction of the gut tube that involves ONLY the circular muscle — the longitudinal muscle is NOT involved. Its primary purpose is to mix and break down food particles, not to propel them. It occurs mainly in the small and large intestines.

questions.q: What initiates peristalsis in the GI tract?
options: Chemical composition of the chyme acting on chemoreceptors
Hormonal signals from the pancreas
Distention (stretching) of the gut wall
Sympathetic nerve stimulation
Temperature changes within the lumen
questions.explain: According to the lecture, peristalsis is initiated mainly by distention (stretching) of the gut wall. While chemoreceptors and hormones can modulate GI motility, the primary trigger for peristalsis is mechanical stretching from luminal contents. Sympathetic stimulation is inhibitory to motility.

questions.q: In which sites does peristalsis occur?
options: Only in the esophagus
Esophagus, distal stomach, small intestine, and large intestine
Small intestine and large intestine only
Stomach and small intestine only
Esophagus and small intestine only
questions.explain: The lecture states peristalsis occurs in the esophagus, the distal stomach, the small intestine, and the large intestine. Segmentation (not peristalsis) is confined to the small and large intestines. The esophagus uses peristalsis exclusively for propulsion.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of GI tract movement?
options: Translocation of food from one location to another
Mechanical breakdown of food particles
Mixing food to renew contact with secreting and absorbing surfaces
Chemical digestion of proteins by proteolytic enzymes
All of the above are functions of GI movement
questions.explain: The three functions of GI movement are translocation, mechanical breakdown, and mixing for renewal of surface contact. Chemical digestion by enzymes is a function of secretory glands, not of the movement itself. Movement and chemical digestion are distinct processes.

questions.q: What does intrinsic control of GI motility involve?
options: Sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems acting on gut smooth muscle
GI peptide hormones secreted in response to luminal chemical stimulants
The enteric nervous system, modulated by gut wall stretching and chyme composition
Central nervous system commands transmitted via the vagus nerve only
Hormonal signals from the pancreas and liver
questions.explain: Intrinsic control is mediated by the enteric nervous system (ENS) and is triggered by stretching of the GI tract wall and by substances in the chyme. GI hormones and the autonomic nervous system represent extrinsic control. The vagus nerve is a component of extrinsic parasympathetic control, not intrinsic.

questions.q: How do the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems differ in their effects on GI motility?
options: Both are excitatory; they differ only in speed of action
Sympathetic is excitatory; parasympathetic is inhibitory
Sympathetic is inhibitory; parasympathetic is excitatory
Both are inhibitory under resting conditions
Sympathetic controls secretion; parasympathetic controls motility only
questions.explain: The sympathetic nervous system is inhibitory to GI motility (reducing it), while the parasympathetic nervous system is excitatory (increasing motility and secretion). This is a classic exam distinction — the "fight or flight" sympathetic response slows digestion, while the "rest and digest" parasympathetic response promotes it.

questions.q: The enteric nervous system (ENS) is sometimes called the 'little brain in the gut' because it:
options: Is directly controlled by the cerebral cortex for all digestive reflexes
Contains approximately 100 million neurons and operates as an independent integrative system
Contains more neurons than the human brain
Is formed entirely of sensory neurons that relay signals to the CNS
Consists only of the myenteric plexus
questions.explain: The ENS contains about 100 million neurons — more than a mouse brain — and is organised as an independent integrative system capable of coordinating GI reflexes without CNS input. It contains more neurons than a mouse brain, not the human brain (which has ~86 billion neurons). It consists of both myenteric and submucosal plexuses.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly lists the three types of neurons found in the enteric nervous system?
options: Sensory neurons, motor neurons, and glial cells
Mechanoreceptors, chemoreceptors, and thermoreceptors
Sensory neurons, interneurons, and motor neurons
Preganglionic, postganglionic, and interneurons
Adrenergic, cholinergic, and serotonergic neurons
questions.explain: The ENS contains sensory neurons (detecting mechanical, chemical, and thermal stimuli), interneurons (coordinating reflexes and motor programs), and motor neurons (excitatory or inhibitory, targeting muscle, secretory epithelium, endocrine cells, and blood vessels). Mechanoreceptors, chemoreceptors, and thermoreceptors are subtypes of sensory neurons, not the three broad neuron types.

questions.q: The ENS myenteric plexus consists of two named components. What is a 'plexus' in this context?
options: A gland that secretes digestive enzymes
A layer of smooth muscle
A network of neurons
A bundle of blood vessels
A mucous membrane lining
questions.explain: According to the lecture, a plexus is defined as 'a network of neurons.' Both the myenteric plexus (between muscle layers) and the submucosal plexus (in submucosa) are neural networks, not glands, muscles, or blood vessels.

questions.q: In Hirschsprung's disease, which segment of the large intestine is abnormal, and what is the clinical consequence?
options: The proximal (normal) colon lacks nerve cells and becomes narrow
Nerve cells fail to form in part or all of the large intestine, causing the affected segment to block waste and the normal colon proximal to it to swell
Nerves form in excess, causing uncontrolled propulsion and diarrhoea
The small intestine loses its nerve supply, blocking absorption
The rectum hypertrophies and prevents defecation
questions.explain: In Hirschsprung's disease, nerves fail to form in the affected (aganglionic) segment of the large intestine, so waste cannot pass through it. The normal colon proximal to the blockage swells with accumulated stool. It is the normal segment that dilates — not the diseased segment.

questions.q: Saliva from the salivary glands serves which two primary roles in the mouth?
options: Solubilises proteins and activates bile production
Lubricates food (mechanical aid) and contains amylase for chemical digestion of starch
Provides HCl for disinfection and mucus for lubrication
Begins fat emulsification and lubricates the esophagus
Contains pepsin for protein digestion and water to dissolve minerals
questions.explain: Saliva serves two roles: it lubricates food to aid mechanical digestion, and it contains salivary amylase which begins chemical digestion of polysaccharides (starch). HCl and pepsin are gastric, not salivary, secretions. Bile emulsifies fat in the small intestine, not the mouth.

questions.q: What is the primary function of the large intestine (hindgut)?
options: Absorption of all food macronutrients
Secretion of digestive enzymes and bicarbonate
Accumulation and dehydration of digestion waste; absorption of water, ions, and some vitamins
Mechanical shaking and mixing of food for enzymatic digestion
Propulsion of food by peristalsis into the small intestine
questions.explain: The large intestine (hindgut/colon) accumulates and dehydrates digestion waste and absorbs water, ions, and some vitamins. Macro-nutrient absorption is the function of the small intestine. The large intestine does not secrete digestive enzymes; this is mainly a pancreatic and small intestinal function.

questions.q: A patient's colon lacks the myenteric plexus in a segment. Which motility type would be most directly impaired in that segment?
options: Chemical digestion, because the plexus releases enzymes
Bile secretion into the intestine
Peristalsis and segmentation, because both depend on ENS coordination of smooth muscle
Only absorption, because the plexus controls mucosal transport
Salivary secretion, because the ENS controls all secretions
questions.explain: The myenteric plexus (Auerbach) coordinates smooth muscle activity for both peristalsis and segmentation. Without it, the smooth muscle of the affected segment cannot receive the neural signals needed for coordinated motility. Chemical digestion uses enzymes from glands, not the plexus; absorption and salivation are not directly controlled by the myenteric plexus.

questions.q: Why does food move through the GI tract even in the absence of gravity, as seen in space?
options: Osmotic pressure gradients drive fluid and food toward the large intestine
Enzymatic activity creates pressure that pushes food along
GI motility is driven entirely by smooth muscle contractions, independent of gravity
Bile secretion creates a pressure gradient that propels food distally
The negative pressure of the thorax draws food into the gut
questions.explain: The lecture specifically notes that food moves down the GI tract in the absence of gravity (e.g., in space), demonstrating that GI mobility is entirely dependent on muscle-driven motility — peristalsis and segmentation — not gravity. Osmotic pressure, enzymatic activity, and bile flow do not drive food translocation.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly compares peristalsis and segmentation?
options: Peristalsis: circular muscle only, mixing; Segmentation: both muscles, propulsion
Peristalsis: both longitudinal and circular, propulsion, aboral; Segmentation: circular only, mixing, non-travelling
Peristalsis: longitudinal only, propulsion; Segmentation: both muscles, mixing
Peristalsis: non-travelling, mixing; Segmentation: travelling, propulsion
Peristalsis and segmentation both use both muscle layers and have the same primary function
questions.explain: Peristalsis uses both longitudinal and circular smooth muscles to produce a travelling wave that propels food aborally. Segmentation uses only circular muscle in an asynchronous, non-travelling pattern whose primary function is mixing and mechanical breakdown. These are the definitive distinctions tested in exams.

questions.q: The pancreas is classified as an accessory digestive organ. Which of the following statements about it is correct according to this lecture?
options: It produces bile salts to emulsify fats
It secretes HCl and pepsin for protein digestion
It secretes hydrolytic enzymes and bicarbonate; it also has nondigestive endocrine functions
It stores and concentrates bile between meals
It is part of the hindgut and absorbs vitamins
questions.explain: The pancreas secretes hydrolytic enzymes (to digest carbohydrates, fats, proteins, and nucleic acids) and bicarbonate (to neutralise gastric acid in the small intestine). The lecture also notes it has nondigestive endocrine functions (e.g., insulin secretion). Bile salts are from the liver; HCl and pepsin are from the stomach; bile storage is the gallbladder.

questions.q: A student claims the headgut performs absorption of amino acids. Based on the lecture, this is incorrect because:
options: The headgut absorbs fats, not amino acids
The headgut only receives and begins processing food; no absorption occurs there
Amino acid absorption requires bile from the liver, which is only available in the hindgut
The headgut lacks a lumen through which nutrients could pass
Amino acids are absorbed only by the large intestine
questions.explain: The headgut (mouth and pharynx) is responsible only for food intake and primary mechanical processing — chewing, lubrication, and initiation of starch digestion. No absorption occurs in the headgut. Absorption of amino acids (and all other food products) is a function of the small intestine (midgut).

lectures.title: Lecture 5: GI2: Digestion — Mouth, Esophagus, Stomach, Pancreas & Liver

questions.q: Which cells in the salivary glands are responsible for secreting mucins that make saliva viscous?
options: Serous cells
Mucous cells
Chief cells
Parietal cells
ECL cells
questions.explain: Mucous cells secrete mucins (glycoproteins) that combine with the watery serous secretion to produce viscous saliva. Serous cells secrete the watery component containing electrolytes, amylase, and antibacterial proteins. Chief and parietal cells are found in the stomach, not in salivary glands.

questions.q: Salivary amylase breaks starch into which of the following products?
options: Glucose and fructose
Sucrose and lactose
Maltose, maltotriose, and oligosaccharides
Fatty acids and glycerol
Amino acids and dipeptides
questions.explain: Salivary amylase cleaves starch into maltose (2 glucose units), maltotriose (3 glucose units), and oligosaccharides (more than 3 glucose units). It does not produce monosaccharides directly, nor does it act on lipids or proteins.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes defensins?
options: Hydrophobic proteins secreted exclusively by immune cells
Amphipathic cationic proteins secreted by immune and epithelial cells
Anionic lipids that neutralise bacterial toxins
Glycoproteins that bind vitamin B12
Enzymes that digest bacterial cell walls via peptide hydrolysis
questions.explain: Defensins are amphipathic (both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions) and cationic, and they are secreted by both immune cells and epithelial cells. They disrupt pathogen membranes and block viral receptors. Describing them as exclusively produced by immune cells, or as anionic, is incorrect.

questions.q: Sympathetic stimulation of salivary glands produces which type of saliva?
options: A large volume of watery, amylase-rich saliva
No saliva at all — sympathetic stimulation fully inhibits secretion
A small volume of viscous, mucin-rich saliva
A large volume of alkaline, bicarbonate-rich saliva
A moderate volume identical in composition to parasympathetically stimulated saliva
questions.explain: Sympathetic stimulation produces a smaller volume of viscous, mucin-rich saliva compared with the moderate watery secretion induced by parasympathetic stimulation. This reduced volume causes the dry-mouth sensation associated with stress but does not completely stop secretion. Parasympathetic nerves (facial and glossopharyngeal) drive the larger, more watery output.

questions.q: Fear and psychological stress inhibit salivary secretion primarily through which mechanism?
options: Direct blockade of amylase synthesis in serous cells
Reduction of parasympathetic tone and indirect sympathetic effects mediated via the cerebral cortex and salivary centre
Increased gastric acid reflux that damages salivary duct cells
Depletion of mucin stores in mucous cells
Activation of upper esophageal sphincter that diverts saliva into the esophagus
questions.explain: Fear acts as a psychological stimulus processed through the cerebral cortex, which inhibits the salivary centre in the medulla — reducing the parasympathetic drive that normally promotes secretion. Sleep, fatigue, and dehydration also suppress output via this pathway. There is no direct blockade of amylase synthesis or structural damage to duct cells.

questions.q: Which region of the pharynx serves exclusively as a passageway for air and NOT for food or fluids?
options: Oropharynx
Laryngopharynx
Nasopharynx
Hypopharynx
Glossopharynx
questions.explain: The nasopharynx communicates only with the nasal cavity and serves exclusively as an air passageway; food and fluids do not normally pass through it. The oropharynx and laryngopharynx are common passageways for both air and ingested material.

questions.q: During the swallowing reflex, what prevents food from entering the trachea?
options: The uvula seals the glottis from above
The upper esophageal sphincter closes the tracheal opening
The epiglottis folds over the glottis to seal the larynx and trachea
The vocal cords open widely to divert food into the esophagus
The soft palate descends to cover the glottis
questions.explain: During swallowing, the epiglottis tilts posteriorly and inferiorly to cover the glottis (the opening into the larynx and trachea), preventing aspiration of food. Simultaneously, the soft palate elevates to close the nasal passage. The upper esophageal sphincter relaxes to allow the bolus to enter the esophagus, not to seal the trachea.

questions.q: GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) results from failure of which structure?
options: Upper esophageal sphincter
Pyloric sphincter
Sphincter of Oddi
Lower esophageal sphincter
Pharyngeal constrictor muscles
questions.explain: GERD is caused by failure of the lower esophageal (cardiac) sphincter to close after food enters the stomach, allowing acidic gastric contents to reflux into the esophagus. The upper esophageal sphincter controls entry from the laryngopharynx into the esophagus. The pyloric sphincter controls stomach emptying into the duodenum.

questions.q: What is the role of rugae in the stomach?
options: Secretion of HCl during the cephalic phase
Absorption of vitamin B12 in the fundus
Large mucosal folds that permit expansion and distension of the empty stomach
Mechanical churning of food via smooth muscle contractions
Filtering of chyme before entry into the duodenum
questions.explain: Rugae are large folds in the gastric mucosa of the empty stomach that allow the organ to greatly expand as food fills it. They are structural features, not active secretory or absorptive units. Mechanical churning is performed by the muscularis externa, not the rugae.

questions.q: Which gastric cell type secretes BOTH pepsinogen AND gastric lipase?
options: Parietal cells
G cells
ECL cells
Chief (zymogenic) cells
Mucous neck cells
questions.explain: Chief cells (also called zymogenic cells) secrete pepsinogen (the precursor to pepsin) and gastric lipase. Parietal cells secrete HCl and intrinsic factor. G cells secrete gastrin. ECL cells secrete histamine. Mucous neck cells secrete mucus and bicarbonate.

questions.q: G cells in the stomach are located primarily in which anatomical region?
options: Fundus
Body (corpus)
Pyloric antrum
Cardia
Esophageal hiatus
questions.explain: G cells are located primarily in the pyloric antrum of the stomach (and also in the duodenum and pancreas). Their secretion of gastrin is stimulated by stomach distension and protein digestion products. They are not uniformly distributed throughout the stomach.

questions.q: At what pH is pepsinogen converted to pepsin, and at what pH does pepsin show maximal activity?
options: Converted at pH > 7; maximal activity at pH 5
Converted at pH < 2; maximal activity at pH 4
Converted at pH < 5; maximal activity at pH ~2
Converted at pH < 5; maximal activity at pH 5
Converted at pH 7; maximal activity at pH 7
questions.explain: Pepsinogen is converted to pepsin when the pH drops below 5, but pepsin's maximal enzymatic activity occurs at a more acidic pH of approximately 2. These are two distinct pH thresholds that are commonly confused in exams. Additionally, pepsin can autocatalytically convert more pepsinogen to pepsin.

questions.q: Intrinsic factor secreted by parietal cells is essential for absorption of which vitamin, and in which intestinal segment does absorption occur?
options: Vitamin C; absorbed in the jejunum
Vitamin D; absorbed in the duodenum
Vitamin B12; absorbed in the ileum
Vitamin K; absorbed in the colon
Vitamin B12; absorbed in the duodenum
questions.explain: Intrinsic factor is a glycoprotein secreted by parietal cells that binds vitamin B12 (cobalamin), which is required for RBC production. The intrinsic factor-B12 complex is absorbed specifically in the ileum. Damage to parietal cells or the ileum results in pernicious anaemia.

questions.q: In parietal cell HCl secretion, which apical transporter directly pumps H⁺ into the gastric lumen?
options: Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase
Cl⁻/HCO₃⁻ exchanger
H⁺/K⁺-ATPase (proton pump)
Carbonic anhydrase
Na⁺/H⁺ exchanger
questions.explain: The H⁺/K⁺-ATPase (proton pump) on the apical membrane of parietal cells actively exports H⁺ into the gastric lumen in exchange for K⁺ uptake — this is the key step in HCl secretion. Carbonic anhydrase generates H⁺ and HCO₃⁻ from CO₂ and water but is located basolaterally. The Cl⁻/HCO₃⁻ exchanger provides Cl⁻ to the lumen but does not pump H⁺.

questions.q: During parietal cell HCl secretion, bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) exits the cell via which membrane?
options: Apical membrane into the gastric lumen
Basolateral membrane into the blood (the "alkaline tide")
Apical membrane via the Cl⁻/HCO₃⁻ exchanger
Basolateral membrane via the proton pump
Lateral membrane back into the gastric gland lumen
questions.explain: HCO₃⁻ generated by carbonic anhydrase inside parietal cells exits through the basolateral membrane into the blood, causing the post-prandial alkaline tide seen in venous blood draining the stomach. The apical Cl⁻/HCO₃⁻ exchanger moves Cl⁻ into the lumen (not HCO₃⁻ into the lumen).

questions.q: Which of the following is the signal type by which histamine stimulates parietal cell HCl secretion?
options: Endocrine — histamine enters the bloodstream to reach parietal cells
Neurotransmitter — released from vagal nerve terminals
Paracrine — released locally by ECL cells to act on nearby parietal cells
Autocrine — released by parietal cells themselves
Hormonal — released by G cells in the antrum
questions.explain: Histamine is produced by enterochromaffin-like (ECL) cells within the gastric gland epithelium and acts in a paracrine manner — diffusing locally to bind histamine receptors on nearby parietal cells. Gastrin is the endocrine stimulus (from G cells via bloodstream); acetylcholine is the neurotransmitter stimulus (from vagal nerve terminals).

questions.q: Cimetidine reduces gastric acid secretion effectively even though it blocks only one of the three stimuli. Why?
options: Cimetidine directly inhibits the H⁺/K⁺-ATPase proton pump
The three stimuli are synergistic, so blocking histamine reduces the amplified response to all three
Cimetidine also blocks acetylcholine receptors on parietal cells
Histamine is the only stimulus active during the intestinal phase
Cimetidine prevents gastrin release from G cells
questions.explain: The three stimuli — histamine, acetylcholine, and gastrin — are synergistic, meaning they amplify each other's effects on parietal cells. Blocking histamine receptors with cimetidine therefore reduces the overall synergistic response to all three stimuli, making H₂ blockers highly effective. Cimetidine does not block the proton pump directly (that is the mechanism of proton pump inhibitors like omeprazole).

questions.q: Which phase of gastric secretion is initiated by the sight, smell, taste, or thought of food?
options: Intestinal phase
Gastric phase
Cephalic phase
Enteric phase
Pyloric phase
questions.explain: The cephalic phase is controlled by the nervous system and is triggered by sensory and cognitive stimuli — sight, smell, taste, thought, chewing, and swallowing — even before food enters the stomach. The gastric phase begins when food distends the stomach. The intestinal phase occurs when partially digested food enters the duodenum.

questions.q: How does H. pylori survive the extremely acidic environment of the stomach?
options: It secretes intrinsic factor to neutralise local HCl
It shelters inside parietal cells, where the pH is neutral
It produces large quantities of mucus that dilute stomach acid
It uses urease to generate ammonia, buffering the local pH
It expresses H⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibitors that suppress HCl secretion
questions.explain: H. pylori survives the extreme gastric pH primarily by producing urease, which converts urea to ammonia and CO₂, creating a locally neutral microenvironment around the bacterium. The lecture notes that ~50% of the world population is affected by H. pylori, which increases the risk of gastric ulcers. The other options are not described mechanisms for H. pylori survival.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the stomach?
options: Acting as a food reservoir
Fragmenting food by mechanical action
Mixing food with secretions to form chyme
Absorbing the majority of dietary nutrients
Emptying gastric contents into the small intestine at a controlled rate
questions.explain: The stomach's four main functions are acting as a reservoir, mechanical fragmentation, chemical digestion (forming chyme), and controlled emptying via the pyloric sphincter. The majority of nutrient absorption occurs in the small intestine, not the stomach. The stomach absorbs very few substances (e.g., some alcohol and aspirin).

questions.q: What is the primary function of NaHCO₃-rich fluid secreted by pancreatic duct epithelial cells?
options: Activating trypsinogen to trypsin in the pancreatic duct
Providing the enzyme complement for protein digestion
Neutralising acidic chyme from the stomach and providing an optimal pH for pancreatic enzymes
Emulsifying dietary fats before pancreatic lipase can act
Concentrating bile entering from the common bile duct
questions.explain: Duct epithelial cells secrete a large volume of NaHCO₃-rich, watery solution that neutralises the acidic chyme arriving from the stomach and establishes the alkaline pH optimal for pancreatic enzyme activity. It also flushes out sticky enzyme secretions from acinar cells. Emulsification is performed by bile salts, and enzyme secretion is the role of acinar cells.

questions.q: Which cell type in the exocrine pancreas secretes digestive enzymes?
options: Duct epithelial cells
Islet beta cells
Acinar cells
Chief cells
ECL cells
questions.explain: Acinar cells are the enzyme-secreting cells of the exocrine pancreas, producing a small volume of enzyme-rich juice. Duct epithelial cells secrete the large volume of bicarbonate-rich fluid. Islet beta cells are endocrine, secreting insulin. Chief cells and ECL cells belong to the gastric mucosa.

questions.q: Which intestinal enzyme initiates the activation cascade of pancreatic zymogens?
options: Pepsin
Trypsin
Enteropeptidase (enterokinase)
Chymotrypsin
Salivary amylase
questions.explain: Enteropeptidase (also called enterokinase), a transmembrane brush-border enzyme of the duodenum and jejunum, converts trypsinogen to active trypsin. Trypsin then activates all other pancreatic zymogens (chymotrypsinogen, proelastase, procarboxypeptidases, etc.) and can also autocatalytically activate more trypsinogen. Pepsin is a gastric enzyme unrelated to this cascade.

questions.q: Most pancreatic proteases are secreted as inactive zymogens. What is the primary physiological reason for this?
options: Zymogens are more stable for long-distance transport via the bloodstream
Inactive forms allow selective activation only in the presence of bile salts
To prevent autodigestion of the pancreas before enzymes reach the duodenum
Zymogens are required to cross the brush-border membrane of enterocytes
The pancreas lacks the co-factors needed for enzyme activity
questions.explain: Secreting proteases as inactive zymogens protects the pancreas from self-digestion. Active proteases released prematurely within the pancreas can destroy pancreatic tissue — a mechanism underlying acute pancreatitis. Activation occurs safely in the duodenal lumen after enteropeptidase initiates the cascade.

questions.q: The liver is described as having both digestive and non-digestive functions. Which of the following is its MAJOR digestive (exocrine) function?
options: Gluconeogenesis and glycogen storage
Synthesis of clotting factors
Bile formation and secretion
Metabolism of steroid hormones
Urea synthesis from ammonia
questions.explain: Bile formation is the major exocrine (digestive) function of the liver. Hepatocytes secrete approximately 1 L of bile per day into bile canaliculi, which eventually drain into the duodenum. The other options (gluconeogenesis, clotting factor synthesis, steroid metabolism, urea synthesis) are non-digestive metabolic functions of the liver.

questions.q: What happens to bile between meals?
options: It continues to flow directly into the duodenum through the sphincter of Oddi
It is diverted into the gallbladder via the cystic duct, where it is stored and concentrated
It is reabsorbed by hepatocytes via bile canaliculi for re-synthesis
It accumulates in the common bile duct until the next meal
It enters the pancreatic duct and mixes with pancreatic juice
questions.explain: Between meals, the sphincter of Oddi is contracted, so bile produced by hepatocytes is diverted through the cystic duct into the gallbladder. The gallbladder stores bile and concentrates it by removing water. During a meal (triggered by CCK and other signals), the gallbladder contracts and the sphincter of Oddi relaxes, releasing bile into the duodenum.

questions.q: Bile salts are described as amphipathic. Which two functions does this amphipathic property enable?
options: Neutralising stomach acid and activating pancreatic zymogens
Emulsifying dietary fats and solubilising biliary cholesterol to prevent gallstones
Binding vitamin B12 and transporting it to the ileum
Killing oral bacteria and neutralising salivary pH
Breaking down triglycerides directly and absorbing fat-soluble vitamins
questions.explain: Bile salts' amphipathic structure (both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions) allows them to (A) emulsify large fat globules into small droplets, increasing the surface area for pancreatic lipase; and (B) keep cholesterol and phospholipids solubilised in bile, thereby preventing gallstone formation. Bile salts do not have enzymatic activity and do not bind vitamin B12.

questions.q: Bilirubin, the primary bile pigment, is derived from which molecule, and what clinical sign results from its accumulation in the blood?
options: Cholesterol; hypercholesterolaemia
Haemoglobin; jaundice
Myoglobin; cyanosis
Albumin; oedema
Haemoglobin; gallstones
questions.explain: Bilirubin is a breakdown product of haemoglobin and gives bile its characteristic yellow-green colour. Elevated bilirubin in the blood causes jaundice (yellowing of the skin and sclera). It is an excretory product rather than a digestive secretion, distinguishing it from bile salts.

questions.q: A patient has significantly decreased secretion of bile salts. Which of the following consequences is most directly predicted by this finding?
options: Increased pepsin activity in the stomach
Failure of enteropeptidase to activate trypsinogen
Impaired fat emulsification and increased risk of cholesterol gallstone formation
Deficiency of intrinsic factor leading to vitamin B12 malabsorption
Elevated pancreatic enzyme secretion from acinar cells
questions.explain: Bile salts serve two key roles: emulsifying dietary fats (enabling pancreatic lipase to act) and keeping biliary cholesterol solubilised. Decreased bile salt secretion impairs fat digestion and allows cholesterol to precipitate, forming cholesterol gallstones. This is one of the key mechanisms of gallstone formation described in the lecture.

questions.q: Comparing salivary amylase and pancreatic amylase, which statement is most accurate?
options: Only salivary amylase can break down starch; pancreatic amylase acts solely on proteins
Both enzymes break down starch to the same products, but pancreatic amylase is secreted as a zymogen requiring activation
Both enzymes break down starch to maltose, maltotriose, and oligosaccharides, but pancreatic amylase contributes far more to total starch digestion
Salivary amylase requires an alkaline pH while pancreatic amylase requires an acidic pH
Pancreatic amylase is secreted by duct epithelial cells; salivary amylase by mucous cells
questions.explain: Both salivary and pancreatic amylase hydrolyse starch to maltose, maltotriose, and oligosaccharides. However, pancreatic amylase is the dominant enzyme for starch digestion in the small intestine, contributing far more than salivary amylase. Unlike proteases, pancreatic amylase is NOT secreted as a zymogen — it is secreted in active form. Pancreatic amylase comes from acinar cells, not duct epithelial cells.

lectures.title: Lecture 6: GI3: Absorption — Small Intestine & Large Intestine

questions.q: Without any anatomical specialisations, the gross surface area of the human small intestine would be approximately:
options: 3.3 m²
200 m²
20 m²
questions.explain: The unmodified gross surface area of the small intestine is ~0.33 m² (equivalent to 7–8 book pages). The true absorptive surface reaches ~200 m² only after amplification by circular folds (×3), villi (×10), and microvilli (×20). 3.3 m² and 20 m² are plausible distractors but are not documented values.

questions.q: Which of the following dietary carbohydrates is classified as indigestible and is excreted in feces?
options: Cellulose
Sucrose
Lactose
Maltose
Starch
questions.explain: Cellulose is a dietary fibre; humans lack the enzyme to cleave its β-1,4 glycosidic bonds. Sucrose, lactose, and maltose are digestible disaccharides, and starch is a digestible polysaccharide — all are broken down and absorbed.

questions.q: Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT) reduces diarrhoea mortality by ~93%. Which mechanism is the primary reason it works?
options: Glucose and Na⁺ are co-transported via SGLT1, and water follows osmotically
Glucose stimulates secretion of anti-diarrhoeal hormones that seal tight junctions
KCl replaces lost potassium and stops intestinal secretion
Sodium citrate alkalinises the lumen, killing pathogenic bacteria
Glucose is metabolised to ATP which powers Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase directly
questions.explain: ORT exploits SGLT1: glucose drives coupled Na⁺ entry into absorptive cells even during active diarrhoea, and water follows by osmosis. KCl and sodium citrate maintain electrolyte balance but are not the primary mechanism for water recovery.

questions.q: Vitamins A, D, E, and K are absorbed by which mechanism?
options: Passive diffusion incorporated into mixed micelles along with dietary lipids
Na⁺-dependent carrier proteins in the brush border
Binding to intrinsic factor, then receptor-mediated endocytosis
Active transport driven by inward H⁺ flux via PEPT1
Aquaporin-mediated facilitated diffusion
questions.explain: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are hydrophobic and are incorporated into mixed micelles together with lipid digestion products, then passively diffuse into absorptive cells. Na⁺-dependent carriers are for free amino acids; intrinsic factor is unique to B12; PEPT1 handles di/tripeptides.

questions.q: At HIGH dietary calcium intake, Ca²⁺ absorption in the small intestine occurs via which mechanism and location?
options: Paracellular passive diffusion through tight junctions throughout the small intestine
Transcellular active transport regulated by calcitriol in the duodenum only
Facilitated diffusion via calbindin across the entire intestinal mucosa
Ca²⁺-ATPase-driven active transport at the brush border of the jejunum
Endocytosis of Ca²⁺-containing vesicles in the ascending colon
questions.explain: When dietary Ca²⁺ is high, absorption occurs by paracellular passive diffusion through tight junctions throughout the entire small intestine — a non-regulated, low-effort route. The transcellular route (Ca²⁺ channels → calbindin → Ca²⁺-ATPase, regulated by calcitriol) is activated when Ca²⁺ intake is LOW.

questions.q: A researcher specifically blocks SGLT1 in an animal model. Which absorption process would be MOST directly impaired?
options: Glucose and galactose uptake from the intestinal lumen
Fructose uptake, since fructose competes with glucose for SGLT1
Dipeptide and tripeptide uptake via PEPT1
Long-chain fatty acid packaging into chylomicrons
Transcellular Ca²⁺ entry via brush-border Ca²⁺ channels
questions.explain: SGLT1 specifically co-transports glucose and galactose with Na⁺. Blocking it directly impairs these two monosaccharides. Fructose uses a separate passive/facilitated transporter independent of SGLT1. PEPT1, chylomicron packaging, and Ca²⁺ channels are entirely distinct systems.

questions.q: Which segment of the small intestine constitutes approximately 5% of its total length?
options: Ileum
Duodenum
Jejunum
Cecum
Sigmoid colon
questions.explain: The duodenum is only ~26 cm long, representing about 5% of the small intestine's total length. The jejunum accounts for ~40% and the ileum the remainder. The cecum and sigmoid colon are parts of the large intestine, not the small intestine.

questions.q: Which anatomical feature of the small intestine provides the LARGEST per-unit surface-area amplification?
options: Circular folds
Microvilli
Villi
Tight junctions
Aquaporins
questions.explain: Microvilli multiply the surface area by ×20, which is the largest single multiplier. Villi contribute ×10 and circular folds ×3. Students commonly reverse microvilli and villi — microvilli are the smallest structures yet give the biggest amplification per unit.

questions.q: Dipeptides and tripeptides are the MAJOR pathway for protein absorption. Which transporter and driving ion are responsible for their uptake at the apical membrane?
options: SGLT1; downhill Na⁺ flux
PEPT1; inward H⁺ flux
A Na⁺-dependent AA transporter; Na⁺ gradient
A basolateral facilitated diffusion carrier; no ion coupling
A Ca²⁺-ATPase; ATP hydrolysis
questions.explain: PEPT1 is an H⁺-coupled peptide transporter: the inward H⁺ gradient (generated by Na⁺/H⁺ exchange at the brush border) drives di/tripeptide uptake. SGLT1 (Na⁺-coupled) handles glucose/galactose; Na⁺-dependent transporters handle free AAs; facilitated diffusion mediates basolateral AA exit.

questions.q: What is the role of mixed micelles in lipid absorption?
options: They directly fuse with absorptive cell membranes and inject lipids
They keep hydrophobic lipid products in aqueous solution and replenish free molecules at the brush border
They hydrolyse triglycerides into fatty acids and monoglycerides
They package long-chain fatty acids into chylomicrons for lymph transport
They activate SGLT1 to co-transport lipids with Na⁺
questions.explain: Mixed micelles (bile salts, phospholipids, fatty acids, monoglycerides) solubilise hydrophobic lipids and are in dynamic equilibrium with free molecules in solution. It is the free molecules — not the micelles themselves — that passively diffuse into absorptive cells. Micelles do not fuse with or enter the cell membrane.

questions.q: A 14-carbon saturated fatty acid is absorbed by the small intestine. Which route does it take after entering the absorptive cell?
options: Directly into the portal vein as a free fatty acid
Packaged into chylomicrons → expelled into central lacteal → lymph
Stored permanently in lipid droplets within the absorptive cell
Converted to glucose via gluconeogenesis in the smooth ER
Transported via aquaporins into the interstitial fluid
questions.explain: A 14-carbon fatty acid is long-chain (>12C). Long-chain fatty acids are re-synthesised into triglycerides in the ER, packaged with apolipoproteins into chylomicrons in the Golgi, and expelled by exocytosis into the central lacteal (lymph). Short-chain (<12C) fatty acids go directly to the portal vein.

questions.q: Along the intestine, where is the rate of sodium (Na⁺) absorption HIGHEST?
options: Duodenum
Jejunum
Ileum
Ascending colon
Descending colon
questions.explain: Na⁺ is absorbed along the entire intestine, but the highest absorption rate occurs in the jejunum. Rates are progressively slower in the ileum and colon. The jejunum is also the primary site of water absorption, which is osmotically driven by Na⁺.

questions.q: When dietary calcium intake is LOW, calcium is absorbed by which route and in which specific location?
options: Paracellular (passive) diffusion throughout the entire small intestine
Transcellular (active) transport in the duodenum and upper jejunum
Transcellular (active) transport in the ileum and large intestine
Paracellular (passive) diffusion in the ascending colon
Aquaporin-mediated transport in the jejunum
questions.explain: When dietary Ca²⁺ is low, the body upregulates transcellular active absorption specifically in the duodenum and upper jejunum via three steps: Ca²⁺ channels at the brush border, calbindin shuttling through the cytosol, and Ca²⁺-ATPase export at the basolateral membrane. High dietary Ca²⁺ uses the simpler paracellular passive route.

questions.q: Fat-soluble nutrients that form large chylomicrons drain into the venous circulation via which route?
options: Portal vein → liver directly
Hepatic vein → inferior vena cava
Central lacteal → lymphatic vessels → venous circulation
Superior mesenteric vein → portal vein
Inferior vena cava directly via tight junctions
questions.explain: Chylomicrons are too large for blood capillaries and enter the central lacteal. The lymphatic system eventually drains into the venous circulation. Water-soluble nutrients (glucose, amino acids, short-chain fatty acids) use the portal vein and go to the liver first.

questions.q: Which brush-border enzyme hydrolyses sucrose into glucose and fructose?
options: Isomaltase
Maltase
Sucrase
Lactase
Amylase
questions.explain: Sucrase specifically cleaves sucrose into glucose and fructose. Isomaltase acts on oligosaccharides with α-1,6 linkages; maltase on maltose/maltotriose; lactase on lactose. Amylase is a luminal enzyme (salivary/pancreatic), not a brush-border enzyme.

questions.q: What is the PRIMARY energy source that drives glucose uptake from the intestinal lumen into absorptive cells via SGLT1?
options: Hydrolysis of ATP directly by SGLT1
An inward K⁺ gradient established by K⁺ channels
The downhill electrochemical gradient of Na⁺ into the cell
The uphill flux of Cl⁻ out of the cell
Phosphorylation of glucose by hexokinase at the brush border
questions.explain: SGLT1 is a secondary active transporter that does not hydrolyse ATP itself. It exploits the Na⁺ electrochemical gradient maintained by the basolateral Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase — Na⁺ flows downhill into the cell, and this energy drives glucose/galactose uphill. K⁺ and Cl⁻ gradients and hexokinase are not coupled to SGLT1.

questions.q: How do free amino acids exit absorptive cells across the basolateral membrane into the blood?
options: Secondary active transport driven by Na⁺ gradient
Primary active transport by an AA-ATPase
Facilitated diffusion (passive carrier-mediated transport)
Simple diffusion directly through the lipid bilayer
Exocytosis packaged in membrane vesicles
questions.explain: Free AAs exit the basolateral membrane by facilitated diffusion (passive), not active transport. Apical entry of free AAs is Na⁺-dependent (secondary active). This asymmetry is a common exam trap — active on the apical side, passive on the basolateral side.

questions.q: Triglycerides account for approximately what proportion of dietary lipid?
questions.explain: Triglycerides (triacylglycerols) constitute ~90% of dietary lipid. The remaining ~10% includes cholesterol esters, phospholipids, and fat-soluble vitamins. This proportion matters because different lipid classes use similar but not identical absorption pathways.

questions.q: Which enzymes digest triglycerides into free fatty acids and monoglycerides in the GI tract?
options: Salivary amylase and pancreatic amylase
Brush-border peptidases and pancreatic proteases
Gastric lipase and pancreatic lipase
Bile salts and cholesterol esterase
Isomaltase and sucrase
questions.explain: Triglycerides are digested by gastric lipase (stomach) and pancreatic lipase (small intestine lumen) to yield free fatty acids and monoglycerides. Bile salts aid emulsification but are not enzymes. Amylases digest carbohydrates; peptidases digest proteins; bile salts/cholesterol esterase is a classic distractor.

questions.q: In a patient with complete bile duct obstruction (no bile reaching the intestine), which absorption process would be MOST severely compromised?
options: Glucose absorption via SGLT1
Dipeptide absorption via PEPT1
Fat-soluble vitamin absorption (A, D, E, K) via micelle-dependent diffusion
Water absorption by osmosis following Na⁺
Ca²⁺ absorption via the calcitriol-regulated transcellular route
questions.explain: Bile salts are essential for forming mixed micelles, which solubilise hydrophobic lipid digestion products. Without bile, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) and long-chain fatty acids cannot form micelles and cannot be absorbed efficiently. Glucose, dipeptide, water, and transcellular Ca²⁺ absorption do not require bile salts.

questions.q: Water absorption in the small intestine is best described as:
options: Primary active transport driven by ATP hydrolysis at water-ATPases
Secondary active transport coupled directly to Na⁺ entry via SGLT1
Facilitated diffusion through Na⁺-gated channels
Passive osmosis following absorption of osmotically active solutes
Endocytosis of luminal fluid into absorptive cells
questions.explain: Water moves by osmosis, following the absorption of osmotically active solutes (primarily Na⁺, but also glucose and amino acids). It crosses the epithelium through tight junctions (paracellular) and aquaporins on plasma membranes. Water absorption is entirely passive and is maximal in the jejunum, not the duodenum.

questions.q: How many segments does the colon have, and which option lists them correctly?
options: Two: ascending and descending
Three: ascending, transverse, and sigmoid
Three: ascending, transverse, and descending
Four: ascending, transverse, descending, and sigmoid
Five: ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid, and rectal
questions.explain: The colon has exactly four segments: ascending, transverse, descending, and sigmoid. The cecum and rectum are separate components of the large intestine but are NOT segments of the colon. Many students list only three segments — the sigmoid colon is most commonly omitted.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the duodenum?
options: Neutralisation of gastric acid by pancreatic juice
Initiation of digestion by pancreatic enzymes and bile
Secretion of H₂O, electrolytes, and mucus from submucosal intestinal glands
Serving as the major site of nutrient absorption (~40% of SI length)
Some nutrient absorption at its lower portion
questions.explain: The jejunum — not the duodenum — accounts for ~40% of small intestinal length and is the MAJOR site of nutrient absorption. The duodenum performs acid neutralisation, initiates digestion, secretes fluids from submucosal glands, and absorbs some nutrients at its lower part, but represents only 5% of SI length.

questions.q: After dipeptides and tripeptides enter absorptive cells, what happens before their constituent amino acids cross the basolateral membrane?
options: They are re-exported intact into lymph via exocytosis
They are phosphorylated and stored in secretory vesicles
They are re-assembled into larger proteins in the Golgi apparatus
They are converted to free amino acids by intracellular peptidases
They are directly secreted as intact peptides into the portal vein
questions.explain: Intracellular peptidases inside absorptive cells cleave di/tripeptides into free amino acids, which then exit the basolateral membrane by facilitated diffusion into the blood. This is why the portal vein carries free amino acids, not intact peptides.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly compares the absorption efficiency of free amino acids versus dipeptides/tripeptides?
options: Free AAs are absorbed faster and are the major protein absorption pathway
Both use PEPT1 (H⁺-coupled) and are absorbed with equal efficiency
Free AAs use H⁺ coupling; dipeptides/tripeptides use Na⁺ coupling
Dipeptides/tripeptides are absorbed more efficiently and are the major pathway; free AAs are slower
Both are absorbed exclusively by endocytosis in adults
questions.explain: Dipeptides and tripeptides are absorbed faster and more efficiently than free amino acids and represent the major pathway for dietary protein absorption. Free AAs are transported more slowly by Na⁺-dependent apical transporters. PEPT1 (H⁺-coupled) is specific to di/tripeptides; free AAs use a distinct Na⁺-coupled transporter.

questions.q: A brush-border enzyme deficiency prevents hydrolysis of oligosaccharides with α-1,6 glycosidic linkages. Which enzyme is deficient?
options: Lactase; galactose and glucose cannot be released from lactose
Sucrase; fructose and glucose cannot be released from sucrose
Maltase; glucose cannot be released from maltose
Isomaltase; glucose cannot be released from branched oligosaccharides
Amylase; starch cannot be cleaved to oligosaccharides in the lumen
questions.explain: Isomaltase specifically cleaves α-1,6 glycosidic linkages in branched oligosaccharides (the branch points from starch digestion), releasing glucose. Without it, these branched fragments accumulate in the lumen. Maltase acts on α-1,4 linkages in maltose/maltotriose; sucrase on sucrose; lactase on lactose. Amylase is a luminal enzyme, not a brush-border enzyme.

questions.q: Which vitamin requires intrinsic factor for intestinal absorption, distinguishing it from all other water-soluble vitamins?
options: Vitamin C
Thiamine (B1)
Riboflavin (B2)
Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine)
Vitamin B12 (cobalamin)
questions.explain: Vitamin B12 is the only water-soluble vitamin that requires intrinsic factor (secreted by gastric parietal cells) for absorption. Without intrinsic factor, B12 cannot be absorbed and pernicious anaemia results. All other B-complex vitamins and Vitamin C use specific membrane carrier proteins without an additional cofactor.

questions.q: A patient with lactose intolerance experiences bloating and diarrhoea after drinking milk. Which pair of mechanisms best explains these symptoms?
options: Lactase excess → rapid glucose absorption → hypertonic blood → osmotic diarrhoea
Lactose activates mucosal mast cells → histamine release → intestinal secretion
Undigested lactose is directly absorbed → triggers hepatic toxicity → GI symptoms
Lactase breaks down lactose into toxic byproducts that irritate the mucosa
Undigested lactose draws water osmotically; colonic bacteria ferment lactose producing lactic acid and gas
questions.explain: Without lactase, lactose stays in the lumen and draws water by osmosis (fluid accumulation, diarrhoea). Colonic bacteria ferment undigested lactose, producing lactic acid, CO₂, H₂, and methane (bloating, cramps, gas, nausea). Lactose intolerance is enzymatic/osmotic/fermentation-based, not immunological or hepatotoxic.

questions.q: Which segments of the large intestine are primarily responsible for storing feces prior to defecation?
options: Cecum and ascending colon
Ascending and transverse colons
Transverse and descending colons
Rectum and cecum
Descending and sigmoid colons
questions.explain: The descending and sigmoid colons are primarily feces storage sites. The ascending and transverse colons perform the bulk of remaining water and electrolyte absorption and secretion. Once feces reaches the rectum (a separate structure, not a colon segment), the defecation reflex is triggered.

questions.q: Calcitriol (active Vitamin D) regulates transcellular Ca²⁺ absorption. Which steps of the transcellular pathway does it regulate?
options: Only the Ca²⁺ channel at the brush border (Step 1)
Only calbindin expression in the cytosol (Step 2)
Only the Ca²⁺-ATPase at the basolateral membrane (Step 3)
Steps 1 and 3 only (channel and pump, not calbindin)
All three steps: Ca²⁺ channel, calbindin, and Ca²⁺-ATPase
questions.explain: Calcitriol upregulates all three steps of transcellular Ca²⁺ absorption: (1) brush-border Ca²⁺ channels, (2) calbindin synthesis in the cytosol, and (3) the basolateral Ca²⁺-ATPase. A common exam trap is assuming it regulates only one or two steps. Note that high dietary Ca²⁺ bypasses all three steps by using the paracellular route.

lectures.title: Lecture 7: Neuro1: Physiology of Nerve Cells — Ion Channels, Membrane Potential & Action Potentials

questions.q: Which of the following best describes how ion channels move ions across the cell membrane?
options: By passive transport down the electrochemical gradient
By coupling ion movement to ATP hydrolysis
By secondary active transport using Na+ gradients
By symport with glucose molecules
By endocytosis of the channel-ion complex
questions.explain: Ion channels are pore-forming proteins that allow ions to move passively — purely down their electrochemical gradient — at rates exceeding 10^6 ions per second. They never couple to ATP or co-transport mechanisms, which distinguishes them fundamentally from carrier proteins. Options B, C, and D describe active or secondary active transport performed by carrier proteins, not channels. Option E describes endocytosis, an entirely different cellular process.

questions.q: A researcher applies a drug that blocks the Na+/K+-ATPase acutely (over seconds). Which immediate effect is most likely?
options: The resting membrane potential immediately collapses to 0 mV
Action potentials fire spontaneously and continuously
The membrane becomes hyperpolarised to −90 mV
Voltage-gated Na+ channels are directly blocked
The resting potential is largely unchanged in the short term
questions.explain: The Na+/K+-ATPase maintains ion concentration gradients over the long term but is not directly responsible for moment-to-moment resting membrane potential. The resting potential is set by membrane permeabilities and the existing ion gradients, which do not collapse instantly. Only after prolonged pump inhibition do gradients dissipate enough to meaningfully shift the resting potential. Options A and E are incorrect because the gradients do not change immediately. Options B and D are not direct consequences of pump blockade.

questions.q: Which correctly states the orientation of concentrations in the Nernst equation as taught in this lecture?
options: E = (RT/Fz) × ln([ion]in / [ion]out), giving a negative value for K+
E = (RT/Fz) × ln([ion]in / [ion]out), with inside on top
E = (Fz/RT) × ln([ion]out / [ion]in), inverted fraction
E = (RT/z) × log([ion]out / [ion]in), omitting Faraday's constant
E = (RT/Fz) × ln([ion]out / [ion]in), with outside on top
questions.explain: By convention the extracellular side is the reference (0 mV), and the Nernst equation is written with the outside concentration in the numerator: E = (RT/Fz) × ln([ion]out/[ion]in). Placing the inside concentration on top inverts the sign and gives the wrong answer — a common exam error. Options A, B, C, and D all misstate the formula or its components.

questions.q: Using the simplified Nernst equation (58 mV/z × log([ion]out/[ion]in)) and squid axon values (K+: 20 mM outside, 400 mM inside), what is E_K?
options: +75 mV
+55 mV
−55 mV
−75 mV
0 mV
questions.explain: Substituting into the formula: 58/1 × log(20/400) = 58 × log(0.05) = 58 × (−1.30) ≈ −75 mV. The negative value reflects that K+ is much more concentrated inside, so the electrical gradient must be negative inside to balance the outward chemical gradient. +55 mV (Option B) is E_Na, not E_K. 0 mV would mean no gradient exists.

questions.q: The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation differs from the Nernst equation primarily because the GHK equation:
options: Only applies to K+ ions at rest
Weights the contribution of each ion by its relative membrane permeability
Uses intracellular concentration in the numerator
Calculates the equilibrium potential for a single ion
Does not account for ion valence
questions.explain: The GHK equation calculates the actual resting membrane potential by incorporating multiple ions (K+, Na+, Cl−) weighted by their relative membrane permeabilities. At rest, pK : pNa : pCl = 1 : 0.05 : 0.45. The Nernst equation, by contrast, calculates the equilibrium potential for only a single ion, ignoring all others. Options A, C, D, and E all describe features of the Nernst equation or are incorrect statements about GHK.

questions.q: At the resting membrane potential of a typical mammalian neuron, which permeability ratio is correct?
options: pK : pNa : pCl = 1 : 1 : 1
questions.explain: The resting GHK permeability ratios are pK : pNa : pCl = 1 : 0.05 : 0.45, with K+ as the reference (value 1). Because K+ permeability dominates at rest, the resting potential is close to E_K (≈ −75 mV) but slightly depolarised by the small but non-zero permeability to Na+ and Cl−. The other ratios listed either swap values or are physiologically meaningless.

questions.q: Which two conditions are both necessary to establish a membrane potential?
options: A semi-permeable membrane and an ion concentration gradient across it
Presence of ATP and a semi-permeable membrane
An ion concentration gradient and continuous ATP hydrolysis
Voltage-gated channels and a ligand concentration gradient
Carrier proteins and an ion concentration gradient
questions.explain: Two conditions are required: (1) a semi-permeable membrane — different ions have different permeabilities — and (2) an ion concentration gradient across that membrane. Together they create an electrochemical driving force. ATP is not required to establish the potential itself (only to maintain gradients long-term). Voltage-gated channels and carriers play roles in other processes but are not the two necessary conditions stated in the lecture.

questions.q: In a squid giant axon at rest, which statement about Na+ is correct?
options: Both the concentration gradient and electrical gradient drive Na+ inward
Na+ is at electrochemical equilibrium because E_Na equals the resting potential
The electrical gradient opposes Na+ entry but the concentration gradient is negligible
Na+ is more concentrated inside (50 mM) than outside (440 mM)
Na+ efflux through leak channels keeps the membrane at rest
questions.explain: In the squid axon Na+ is 50 mM inside and 440 mM outside, so the concentration gradient drives Na+ inward. The resting membrane potential (inside negative) also electrically attracts positively charged Na+ inward. Both forces act in the same direction, making Na+ strongly driven inward — it is far from equilibrium at rest. Option D reverses the concentrations. Option A is incorrect; E_Na ≈ +55 mV, far from the resting potential of ≈ −75 mV.

questions.q: Tetrodotoxin (TTX) is applied to a neuron. Which result is expected?
options: K+ efflux during repolarisation is blocked, prolonging the AP
The resting membrane potential depolarises immediately to 0 mV
All ion channels are non-selectively blocked
Voltage-gated Na+ channel opening is prevented, abolishing the rising phase of the AP
Ligand-gated Na+ channels are blocked, preventing EPSPs
questions.explain: TTX is a potent and selective blocker of voltage-gated Na+ channels. By preventing Na+ influx during the depolarising phase, it abolishes the rising phase of the action potential — no AP can be generated. It does not block K+ channels (Option A), so repolarisation mechanisms are intact. It does not affect ligand-gated channels or resting potential directly. All other options describe incorrect effects.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly distinguishes ion channels from carrier proteins?
options: Channels can perform primary active transport; carriers cannot
Carriers move ions faster (>10^6 ions/sec); channels are slower
Channels are always passive; carriers can be active or passive
Both channels and carriers require ATP for ion movement
Channels require a ligand to bind before any ions can pass through
questions.explain: The defining rule is that ion channels are always passive — ions move down their electrochemical gradient — while carriers can be either passive (facilitated diffusion) or active (primary or secondary active transport). Ion channels are also much faster than carriers (>10^6 ions/s vs. thousands per second for carriers). Options A and D are incorrect; channels never use ATP. Option B reverses the rate comparison. Option E applies only to ligand-gated channels, not all channels.

questions.q: Which gating mechanism is responsible for opening Na+ channels during the rising phase of an action potential?
options: Ligand-gating by acetylcholine binding
Mechanical deformation of the membrane
Second-messenger gating via cAMP
Osmotic pressure changes across the membrane
Voltage-gating in response to membrane depolarisation
questions.explain: The Na+ channels responsible for the AP rising phase are voltage-gated — they open when the membrane potential depolarises to threshold. The positive-feedback loop of depolarisation opening more voltage-gated Na+ channels drives the regenerative AP. Ligand-gating (Option A) applies to postsynaptic receptor channels, not the AP-generating Na+ channels. Mechanical gating (Option B) is used in sensory mechanoreceptors. Second-messenger gating (Option C) is too slow for the AP timescale.

questions.q: During the repolarisation phase of an action potential, which combination of events occurs?
options: Na+ channels open further; K+ channels close
Na+ channels inactivate AND voltage-gated K+ channels open
Na+ channels simply close (return to closed state); K+ efflux drives repolarisation alone
Na+ channels inactivate; K+ channels also inactivate simultaneously
Cl− channels open and drive the membrane negative
questions.explain: Repolarisation is caused by two simultaneous events: (1) voltage-gated Na+ channels transition to an inactivated state (not simply closed — a distinct conformational state that cannot be immediately reopened by depolarisation), and (2) delayed voltage-gated K+ channels open, allowing K+ to rush out. Option C is a classic exam trap — Na+ channels inactivate rather than simply closing, and both processes contribute to repolarisation.

questions.q: What causes the afterhyperpolarisation phase of an action potential?
options: Voltage-gated K+ channels remaining open longer than necessary after repolarisation
Na+ channels reopening and driving the membrane below rest
The Na+/K+-ATPase rapidly pumping out excess Na+
Cl− channels opening in response to high intracellular Na+
Inactivation of ligand-gated channels at the membrane
questions.explain: After repolarisation, voltage-gated K+ channels remain open briefly longer than needed, allowing continued K+ efflux that drives the membrane below the resting potential — the afterhyperpolarisation. The channels then close and the membrane returns to rest. The Na+/K+-ATPase (Option C) contributes negligibly to this transient event; it operates on a much slower timescale and is primarily for long-term gradient maintenance.

questions.q: The all-or-none property of action potentials means which of the following?
options: Once threshold is crossed, the AP always has the same amplitude and shape regardless of stimulus strength
The AP amplitude increases proportionally with stimulus intensity above threshold
Below threshold a small AP is produced; above threshold a full AP is produced
Every stimulus will eventually produce an AP if given enough time
The AP can only propagate in one direction
questions.explain: The all-or-none law states that once the membrane potential crosses threshold, the resulting AP is always the same amplitude and shape — it does not scale with stimulus intensity. Stimuli below threshold produce only a graded depolarisation, not an AP. Option B is the opposite of all-or-none (that describes a graded response). Option C is incorrect because no partial AP is produced below threshold. Unidirectionality (Option E) is a separate, distinct property.

questions.q: What constitutes the positive-feedback loop responsible for threshold in action potential generation?
options: K+ efflux exceeding Na+ influx, causing progressive depolarisation
Ca2+ influx triggering more Ca2+ release from intracellular stores
Acetylcholine binding opening more Na+ channels until threshold is reached
Membrane depolarisation opening voltage-gated Na+ channels → more Na+ influx → more depolarisation
The Na+/K+-ATPase increasing its pumping rate to create a positive charge excess
questions.explain: The threshold mechanism is a regenerative positive-feedback loop: depolarisation opens voltage-gated Na+ channels → Na+ rushes in → further depolarisation → even more Na+ channels open → runaway depolarisation. The loop is sustained when Na+ influx exceeds K+ efflux. Option A reverses the ion roles. Option B describes Ca2+-induced Ca2+ release (a different process in muscle). Option C involves ligand-gated channels, not voltage-gated ones.

questions.q: During the absolute refractory period, why is it impossible to generate a second action potential?
options: Voltage-gated K+ channels are fully open, clamping the membrane at E_K
All voltage-gated Na+ channels are fully inactivated and cannot be reopened by depolarisation
The Na+/K+-ATPase hyperpolarises the membrane below threshold
Ligand-gated channels are desensitised to neurotransmitter
Membrane resistance is too low to allow depolarisation to spread
questions.explain: During the absolute refractory period, voltage-gated Na+ channels are in the inactivated state — a distinct conformational state from simply closed. Inactivated channels cannot be re-opened by further depolarisation; they must first recover (de-inactivate) by returning to a hyperpolarised potential. This makes threshold effectively infinite. Option A is partially true during repolarisation but is not the primary mechanism for the absolute refractory period.

questions.q: During the relative refractory period, which of the following is true?
options: No action potential can be generated under any circumstances
Na+ channels are fully recovered and the membrane is at normal resting potential
An AP can be generated, but only with a stronger-than-normal stimulus, and the AP will be smaller
K+ channels are fully closed and the membrane sits at exactly the resting potential
Threshold is lower than normal, making the neuron hyperexcitable
questions.explain: During the relative refractory period, Na+ channels are partially (not fully) recovering from inactivation, and the afterhyperpolarisation means the membrane is more negative than rest. A second AP can be generated but requires a stronger stimulus to overcome elevated threshold, and the resulting AP is smaller in amplitude. Option A describes the absolute refractory period. Option E is the opposite — threshold is elevated, not lowered, during the relative refractory period.

questions.q: How does the refractory period contribute to unidirectional AP propagation?
options: The region of membrane just behind the advancing AP is in the absolute refractory period, preventing backward re-excitation
It prevents K+ channels from opening behind the AP wavefront
It causes voltage-gated Na+ channels to open preferentially in the forward direction only
It blocks passive electrotonic spread from occurring in reverse
It increases membrane resistance behind the AP, channelling current forward
questions.explain: As an AP advances, the membrane that was just depolarised enters the absolute refractory period (Na+ channels inactivated). When local current from the AP spreads forward and backward, only the forward membrane can be re-excited — the region just behind it cannot respond because its Na+ channels are fully inactivated. This asymmetry enforces unidirectional propagation. The other options describe mechanisms that do not operate in this context.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly compares passive (electrotonic) propagation with active (AP) propagation?
options: Passive propagation is regenerative; active propagation decrements with distance
Both passive and active propagation spread bidirectionally without decrement
Active propagation is slower than passive propagation over long distances
Passive propagation decrements exponentially; active propagation is regenerative and non-decrementing
Passive propagation is unidirectional; active propagation spreads in all directions
questions.explain: Passive (electrotonic) propagation decrements exponentially with distance (characterised by the length constant λ) and spreads in all directions. Active (AP) propagation is regenerative — voltage-gated channels are re-activated at each patch of membrane — so amplitude does not decay. Additionally, active propagation is unidirectional due to refractoriness. Options A and B reverse these properties. Option C is incorrect: AP propagation is faster over long distances because it does not decay.

questions.q: What is saltatory conduction, and in which type of axon does it occur?
options: AP spreading continuously along the full length of unmyelinated axons
AP regeneration at nodes of Ranvier, appearing to jump between nodes in myelinated axons
Passive electrotonic spread accelerated by high membrane resistance in demyelinated axons
Ion current flowing through myelin sheaths to depolarise distant regions
AP propagation driven by Ca2+ channels at the axon hillock
questions.explain: In myelinated axons, the myelin sheath insulates internodal membrane and increases the effective length constant. APs can only be regenerated at nodes of Ranvier (gaps in myelin), so the AP appears to jump from node to node — saltatory conduction. It is faster and more energetically efficient than continuous conduction in unmyelinated axons of the same diameter. Option C describes what happens in demyelinating disease, not normal saltatory conduction. Option D is incorrect; current does not flow through myelin.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of saltatory conduction over conduction in unmyelinated axons?
options: Higher conduction velocity for the same axon diameter
Lower metabolic cost because fewer ions cross the membrane
The AP amplitude increases progressively along the axon
Less membrane surface area needs to be actively depolarised
More energetically efficient Na+/K+-ATPase usage
questions.explain: AP amplitude does not increase along myelinated axons — the action potential is all-or-none and maintains the same amplitude at each node of Ranvier. The genuine advantages of saltatory conduction are faster velocity (Option A), lower metabolic cost (Options B and E), and less membrane area being actively depolarised (Option D). Option C is false and is the NOT answer because APs do not progressively amplify during conduction.

questions.q: Which ion's influx into the presynaptic terminal triggers neurotransmitter release?
options: Na+ influx through voltage-gated channels
K+ efflux through delayed rectifier channels
Ca2+ influx through voltage-gated Ca2+ channels
Cl− influx through ligand-gated channels
Mg2+ influx through NMDA receptor channels
questions.explain: When the AP depolarises the presynaptic terminal, voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open and Ca2+ flows in (driven strongly by both concentration and electrical gradients). This Ca2+ influx triggers synaptic vesicle fusion with the presynaptic membrane (exocytosis) and neurotransmitter release into the synaptic cleft. Blocking Ca2+ channels abolishes transmitter release even if the AP fires normally. Na+ influx (Option A) generates the AP but does not directly trigger vesicle fusion.

questions.q: A neurotransmitter produces depolarisation at one synapse and hyperpolarisation at another. This is best explained by:
options: The transmitter changing its chemical structure depending on the target cell
Different concentrations of the same transmitter at the two synapses
The presynaptic neuron releasing different transmitters at each terminal
