The type of postsynaptic receptor and the ion channel it gates determining the response
The resting membrane potential of each target cell determining the response sign
questions.explain: The nature of the postsynaptic receptor — not the transmitter identity — determines whether the response is excitatory (EPSP) or inhibitory (IPSP). The same transmitter can open different ion channels at different synapses, producing depolarisation at one and hyperpolarisation at another. The transmitter's chemical identity does not change (Option A), and concentration differences (Option B) do not switch the sign of the response.

questions.q: Which mechanism terminates the action of acetylcholine (ACh) in the synaptic cleft?
options: Re-uptake of ACh by monoamine transporters into the presynaptic terminal
Phosphorylation of ACh by protein kinase A
Enzymatic degradation by acetylcholinesterase
Sequestration of ACh into postsynaptic vesicles
Diffusion of ACh across the postsynaptic membrane into the cytoplasm
questions.explain: Acetylcholine is primarily inactivated by the enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which breaks ACh into acetate and choline within the synaptic cleft. Choline is then taken back up into the presynaptic terminal for re-synthesis. Monoamine transporters (Option A) handle dopamine, serotonin, and noradrenaline — not ACh. Diffusion (Option E) plays a minor role for some peptide transmitters but is not the primary mechanism for ACh.

questions.q: Where does synaptic integration occur in a neuron, and what does it involve?
options: At the cell body; integration of metabolic signals from mitochondria
At the dendrites; summation of voltage-gated channel activity only
At the presynaptic terminal; summation of Ca2+ signals
At the nodes of Ranvier; summation of local electrotonic potentials
At the axon hillock; spatial and temporal summation of EPSPs and IPSPs to reach threshold
questions.explain: Synaptic integration occurs at the axon hillock, which has the highest density of voltage-gated Na+ channels and the lowest threshold for AP initiation. EPSPs and IPSPs from multiple inputs are summed spatially (simultaneous inputs from different locations) and temporally (rapid successive inputs from the same source). Only when the net depolarisation crosses threshold does an AP fire — the neuron acts as a coincidence detector.

questions.q: Which correctly lists all five steps of chemical synaptic transmission in order?
options: Release → Storage → Synthesis → Postsynaptic effects → Inactivation
Synthesis → Storage → Release → Postsynaptic effects → Inactivation
Synthesis → Release → Storage → Inactivation → Postsynaptic effects
Storage → Synthesis → Release → Inactivation → Postsynaptic effects
Synthesis → Storage → Postsynaptic effects → Release → Inactivation
questions.explain: The five sequential steps of chemical synaptic transmission are: (1) Synthesis of neurotransmitter, (2) Storage in synaptic vesicles, (3) Release by Ca2+-triggered exocytosis, (4) Postsynaptic effects (receptor binding → ion channel changes → EPSP/IPSP), and (5) Inactivation (degradation, re-uptake, or diffusion). All other options place steps out of order, which would be physiologically impossible.

questions.q: A neurotransmitter opens K+ channels at the postsynaptic membrane, producing an IPSP. Why is K+ channel opening inhibitory?
options: K+ influx depolarises the membrane, increasing the distance from threshold
K+ efflux hyperpolarises the membrane, moving it further from threshold and making AP initiation harder
K+ efflux directly inactivates voltage-gated Na+ channels
K+ influx clamps the membrane at E_K, which is above threshold
K+ efflux reduces the extracellular K+ concentration, blocking Na+ entry
questions.explain: Opening K+ channels allows K+ to move out of the cell (down its electrochemical gradient at membrane potentials more positive than E_K), hyperpolarising the membrane — moving it further from threshold. This makes it harder for coincident EPSPs to bring the axon hillock to threshold. Option D is incorrect: E_K ≈ −75 mV, which is below (more negative than) threshold, not above it. K+ efflux does not directly inactivate Na+ channels (Option C).

questions.q: The patch-clamp technique is significant in channel biology because it allows:
options: Measurement of ion concentrations inside living cells using fluorescent dyes
Imaging of individual channel protein structures at atomic resolution
Recording of single-channel currents from a tiny membrane patch to study gating and pharmacology
Delivery of genes encoding ion channels directly into neurons
Measurement of neurotransmitter concentrations in the synaptic cleft
questions.explain: The patch-clamp technique, developed by Erwin Neher and Bernd Sakmann (Nobel Prize 1991), seals a glass pipette onto a tiny membrane patch and records currents from individual ion channels. This allows direct observation of channel gating (open/closed transitions), pharmacological effects of drugs, and biophysical properties of channels. Options A, B, D, and E describe other techniques (fluorescence imaging, cryo-EM, gene delivery, electrochemical sensors, respectively).

questions.q: Ca2+ has an intracellular concentration of ~0.0001 mM and an extracellular concentration of ~1–2 mM. At rest (inside ≈ −70 mV), what is the direction of net electrochemical driving force on Ca2+?
options: Strongly outward — both gradients drive Ca2+ out
Weakly inward — concentration gradient in, electrical gradient out, nearly balanced
No net force — Ca2+ is at equilibrium at the resting potential
Weakly outward — electrical gradient slightly exceeds concentration gradient
Strongly inward — both concentration and electrical gradients drive Ca2+ in
questions.explain: Ca2+ is ~10,000× more concentrated outside than inside, creating a massive inward concentration gradient. The interior of the cell is also negative (≈ −70 mV), which electrically attracts the positively charged Ca2+ inward. Both forces act in the same direction — strongly driving Ca2+ into the cell. This is why opening voltage-gated Ca2+ channels at the presynaptic terminal causes rapid Ca2+ influx that triggers vesicle fusion.

questions.q: If demyelination occurs in a previously myelinated axon, which change in conduction is most expected?
options: Conduction velocity increases because more ion channels are now exposed along the axon
The AP amplitude doubles as it can now regenerate continuously at every point
No change occurs because the nodes of Ranvier remain structurally intact
Conduction velocity decreases or conduction fails because the effective length constant is reduced
Saltatory conduction converts to faster and more efficient continuous conduction
questions.explain: Myelin dramatically increases the effective length constant of the axon, allowing AP-generated current to spread far enough to reach the next node of Ranvier and re-excite it. If myelin is lost, the length constant shortens, local currents may fail to reach the next excitable region, and conduction slows drastically or fails (conduction block) — as seen in demyelinating diseases like multiple sclerosis. Option E is the opposite of what actually happens: continuous conduction in unmyelinated axons is slower, not faster.

lectures.title: Lecture 8: Neuro2: Properties of Sensory Cells and Sensory Systems

questions.q: Which of the following correctly defines a sensory receptor?
options: A neuron in the spinal cord that relays signals to the thalamus
A specialized cell, neural or non-neural, that detects a specific environmental stimulus and converts it into an electrical signal
Any cell in the body that can generate an action potential in response to touch
A postsynaptic neuron that integrates signals from multiple sensory pathways
A glial cell that amplifies sensory signals in peripheral nerves
questions.explain: A sensory receptor is defined as a specialized cell — neural or non-neural — that receives a particular environmental stimulus and converts it into an electrical signal the CNS can process. The other options describe interneurons, non-specific excitable cells, or glial cells, none of which match this definition.

questions.q: Which receptor type detects O2 levels, pH, and organic molecules such as glucose?
options: Mechanoreceptors
Photoreceptors
Thermoreceptors
Chemoreceptors
Nociceptors
questions.explain: Chemoreceptors detect chemical stimuli including O2, pH, and organic molecules like glucose. Mechanoreceptors respond to pressure and stretch; photoreceptors to light; thermoreceptors to heat; and nociceptors to noxious tissue-damaging stimuli.

questions.q: In a neural sensory receptor, what is the sequence of events after stimulus application?
options: Receptor potential → neurotransmitter release → action potential in downstream neuron
Action potential → generator potential → receptor potential
Generator potential → action potential in the same cell
Neurotransmitter release → generator potential → action potential
Receptor potential → generator potential → action potential in downstream neuron
questions.explain: In a neural sensory receptor, the stimulus produces a graded generator potential in that same cell, which then triggers an action potential in the same neuron. Non-neural receptors instead produce a receptor potential that triggers neurotransmitter release to excite a downstream neuron.

questions.q: In a non-neural sensory receptor, the receptor potential leads directly to which of the following?
options: An action potential in the same cell
Depolarisation of the thalamus
Neurotransmitter release, which then generates action potentials in downstream neurons
Hyperpolarisation and inhibition of secondary sensory neurons
Opening of voltage-gated Na+ channels in the same receptor cell to fire an AP
questions.explain: Non-neural receptors produce a receptor potential that drives neurotransmitter release. The released neurotransmitter then excites a downstream (postsynaptic) neuron, generating action potentials there. The receptor cell itself does not fire APs.

questions.q: How does the nervous system encode stimulus intensity in sensory pathways?
options: By increasing the amplitude of individual action potentials
By changing the type of ion channel that opens
By increasing the frequency of action potentials
By decreasing the refractory period of the receptor cell only
By switching from generator potential to receptor potential signalling
questions.explain: Stimulus intensity is encoded as the frequency of action potentials (or the amount of neurotransmitter released in non-neural receptors). Action potentials are all-or-none, so their amplitude does not change with stimulus strength. A stronger stimulus increases AP frequency, not AP size.

questions.q: Which of the following accurately describes how stimulus duration is encoded in sensory pathways?
options: Longer stimuli increase the amplitude of each action potential
Longer stimuli produce a higher-frequency but shorter burst of action potentials
The duration of the generator/receptor potential determines the duration of the AP train
Stimulus duration is encoded by the number of receptor types activated, not AP timing
Longer stimuli trigger faster adaptation, reducing the AP train duration
questions.explain: The duration of the generator or receptor potential mirrors stimulus duration, and this determines how long the train of action potentials (or neurotransmitter secretion) lasts. The strength of the potential encodes intensity; the duration encodes how long the stimulus persists.

questions.q: Which neurons cross the midline of the body in the somatosensory pathway?
options: Primary sensory neurons
Secondary sensory neurons
Tertiary sensory neurons
Both primary and secondary sensory neurons
Thalamic relay neurons
questions.explain: Secondary sensory neurons cross the midline (decussate), so that sensations from the left side of the body are processed in the right hemisphere and vice versa. Primary sensory neurons carry signals from the periphery without crossing; tertiary neurons project from thalamus to cortex on the same side.

questions.q: Where do secondary sensory neurons synapse onto tertiary sensory neurons in the somatosensory pathway?
options: Spinal cord
Brainstem
Thalamus
Somatosensory cortex
Hippocampus
questions.explain: Secondary sensory neurons synapse onto tertiary sensory neurons in the thalamus. Tertiary neurons then project to the somatosensory cortex. The thalamus is the key relay station for virtually all sensory information before it reaches the cortex.

questions.q: Which statement about the somatosensory cortex is CORRECT?
options: Each body region has an equal area of cortical representation regardless of sensitivity
The cortical map is fixed at birth and does not change with experience
More sensitive body regions have larger cortical representations, and this size can change with usage
The cortex processes all body sensations on the ipsilateral (same) side
Tertiary sensory neurons bypass the thalamus and project directly to the cortex
questions.explain: More sensitive body regions (e.g., fingertips, lips) have proportionally larger somatosensory cortical areas. Crucially, this mapping is not fixed — it can change with usage, reflecting cortical plasticity. Sensations are processed contralaterally (opposite side) after secondary neurons cross the midline.

questions.q: Piezo2 is a mechanosensitive ion channel responsible for which sensory functions?
options: Noxious heat detection and nociception
Cold sensing and menthol detection
Gentle touch and proprioception
Auditory mechanotransduction in hair cells
Photon detection in photoreceptors
questions.explain: Piezo2 mediates gentle touch and proprioception — the sense of self-movement and body position, described as the 'sixth sense.' The discovery of Piezo channels as touch sensors (along with TRPV1 and TRPM8 as temperature sensors) was recognised by the 2021 Nobel Prize.

questions.q: Which ion channel is specifically activated by noxious heat above 43°C AND by capsaicin from chilli peppers?
options: Piezo2
TRPM8
TRPC1
TRPV1
TRPA1
questions.explain: TRPV1 (a Transient Receptor Potential channel) is activated by noxious heat above 43°C and by capsaicin — the compound responsible for the 'hot' sensation of chilli. TRPM8 senses cold and cooling compounds like menthol; Piezo2 senses touch.

questions.q: TRPM8 is activated by which of the following stimuli?
options: Noxious heat above 43°C and capsaicin
Gentle mechanical touch and proprioception
Temperatures below body temperature, menthol, and eucalyptol
Photons in the visible light spectrum
Osmotic pressure changes and cell stretch
questions.explain: TRPM8 is a cold-sensing TRP channel that responds to temperatures below body temperature and to cooling compounds such as menthol and eucalyptol. Evidence from TRPM8-deficient mice shows they are insensitive to cold. TRPV1, not TRPM8, detects capsaicin and noxious heat.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a correct pairing of a TRP channel with its ligand or stimulus?
options: TRPV1 — capsaicin
TRPM8 — menthol
TRPV1 — noxious heat >43°C
TRPM8 — capsaicin
TRPM8 — eucalyptol
questions.explain: Capsaicin activates TRPV1, not TRPM8. TRPM8 responds to cold and cooling compounds (menthol, eucalyptol). A common exam error is assigning capsaicin to TRPM8 because both involve flavour-linked temperature sensations.

questions.q: The 2021 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for the discovery of which receptors?
options: Voltage-gated Na+ and K+ channels underlying the action potential
GABA receptors and glutamate receptors
TRPV1 and TRPM8 as temperature sensors and Piezo channels as touch/proprioception sensors
Dopamine and serotonin receptors in the reward pathway
Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction
questions.explain: The 2021 Nobel Prize was awarded to David Julius and Ardem Patapoutian for discovering TRPV1 and TRPM8 as temperature sensors and Piezo1/2 as touch/mechanosensation sensors. These discoveries revealed the molecular basis of temperature and touch perception.

questions.q: Nociceptors respond to which categories of stimuli?
options: Only thermal stimuli above 45°C
Only chemical irritants in the bloodstream
Chemical, mechanical, or thermal stimuli that cause or risk tissue damage
Gentle mechanical touch and proprioceptive feedback
Only stimuli that produce inflammation, not mechanical pressure
questions.explain: Nociceptors respond to strong noxious stimuli that cause or have the potential to cause tissue damage, including chemical, mechanical, and thermal stimuli. They are polymodal — responding to multiple stimulus types. They are distinct from touch receptors, which respond to innocuous mechanical stimuli.

questions.q: Nociceptor activation produces which two downstream effects?
options: Conscious temperature perception and proprioceptive feedback
Reflexive protective responses at the spinal cord level and ascending pathways to the cortex producing conscious pain
Neurotransmitter release at the neuromuscular junction and somatic muscle contraction
Inhibition of all ascending sensory pathways and descending pain modulation only
Local vasodilation only and cortical suppression of pain
questions.explain: Nociceptors activate two pathways: (A) reflexive protective responses integrated at the spinal cord (fast, unconscious withdrawal reflexes) and (B) ascending pathways to the cortex that produce conscious pain sensation. Both pathways operate simultaneously.

questions.q: What is the definition of timbre (音色) in the context of sound perception?
options: The total energy carried by a sound wave per unit time
The perceived loudness of a sound measured in decibels
The pitch of a sound determined by its fundamental frequency
The quality that makes instruments sound different even at the same pitch and loudness
The latency between stimulus onset and auditory cortex activation
questions.explain: Timbre (音色) is the complexity component of sound — the characteristic quality that distinguishes one instrument from another even when they play the same note at the same loudness. It is determined by the pattern of overtones (harmonics) in the sound wave.

questions.q: At what sound intensity level can damage to cochlear hair cells occur?
options: Above 40 dB
Above 60 dB
Above 80 dB
Above 100 dB only
Only at 120 dB or above
questions.explain: Sounds above 80 dB can damage hair cells. Normal conversation is approximately 60 dB. The lecture specifically states that 80 dB is the threshold for potential hair cell damage, not 100 dB or 120 dB.

questions.q: The average human ear hears in the range of 20–20,000 Hz, but at which frequency range is hearing most acute?
questions.explain: While the human auditory range spans 20–20,000 Hz, the most acute hearing is at 1–3 kHz (1,000–3,000 Hz). This range corresponds to the frequency of important communication sounds including speech. Sensitivity declines at both lower and higher frequencies.

questions.q: Which statement about inner hair cells (IHC) versus outer hair cells (OHC) in the cochlea is CORRECT?
options: There are 12,000 IHCs arranged in three rows and 3,000 OHCs in one row
OHCs are the primary signal transmitters to the brain; IHCs provide mechanical amplification
There are 3,000 IHCs in one row and 12,000 OHCs in three rows; IHCs are the primary signal transmitters
IHCs and OHCs are equally innervated by afferent cochlear nerve fibers
Both IHCs and OHCs contain prestin for electromotility
questions.explain: There are 3,000 IHCs (one row) and 12,000 OHCs (three rows). IHCs are the primary sensory cells transmitting auditory signals to the brain; OHCs function as mechanical amplifiers. Despite fewer in number, IHCs receive the vast majority of afferent innervation.

questions.q: What is the approximate ratio of afferent cochlear nerve fibers to inner hair cells?
options: 1:10 (one fiber per ten IHCs)
1:1 (one fiber per IHC)
3:1 (three fibers per IHC)
10:1 (ten fibers per IHC)
100:1 (one hundred fibers per IHC)
questions.explain: There are 30,000+ fibers in the cochlear nerve and approximately 3,000 IHCs, giving a nearly 10:1 fiber-to-IHC ratio. This massive innervation reflects the IHC's role as the primary sensory signal transmitter to the brain, ensuring high-fidelity signal transmission.

questions.q: The majority of afferent cochlear nerve fibers innervate which cochlear cell type?
options: Outer hair cells, because there are four times more of them
Inner hair cells, despite there being fewer of them
Support cells of the organ of Corti
Spiral ganglion efferent neurons
Both IHCs and OHCs equally, as each contributes 50% of nerve fibers
questions.explain: Despite OHCs outnumbering IHCs 4:1, the vast majority of afferent cochlear nerve fibers innervate IHCs. Only a sparse number of afferent fibers carry signals from OHCs to the brain; most OHC connections are efferent (descending from brain to OHCs for modulation).

questions.q: What is the function of prestin in outer hair cells?
options: It forms the tip-link mechanotransduction channel that opens upon stereocilia deflection
It releases glutamate onto afferent cochlear nerve fibers in response to sound
It is a transmembrane motor protein that changes shape with membrane voltage changes, causing OHC electromotility and cochlear amplification
It maintains the K+ concentration in endolymph required for hair cell function
It is a TRP channel that converts thermal energy to electrical signals in the cochlea
questions.explain: Prestin is a transmembrane motor protein in OHCs that mechanically contracts and elongates in response to membrane voltage (Vm) changes. This electromotility is the driving force of cochlear amplification — it amplifies the basilar membrane vibration, increasing sensitivity to sound frequencies. Prestin is unique to OHCs, not IHCs.

questions.q: Cochlear amplification by OHCs increases which of the following?
options: The number of IHCs available for signal transmission
The sensitivity to incoming sound frequencies by amplifying basilar membrane vibration
The range of sound frequencies detectable by the human ear beyond 20,000 Hz
The amplitude of action potentials in the cochlear nerve
The concentration of K+ ions in the endolymph
questions.explain: OHC electromotility (driven by prestin) amplifies basilar membrane vibration, increasing the sensitivity to incoming sound frequencies and amplifying the signal reaching IHCs. It does not extend frequency range, change AP amplitude (all-or-none), or increase IHC numbers.

questions.q: Which statement about the mechanotransduction channel in auditory hair cells is accurate according to the lecture?
options: It has been fully characterised as TRPV1
It has been fully characterised as Piezo2
Its molecular identity is still not fully established and is considered the last frontier of sensory neurobiology
It is the same channel as the one mediating gentle touch in skin mechanoreceptors
It was identified in 2021 and awarded the Nobel Prize
questions.explain: The lecture explicitly states that the molecular identity of the mechanotransduction channel in auditory hair cells is 'still not fully established' and is considered the 'last frontier of sensory neurobiology.' TRPV1 and Piezo2 are not the auditory hair cell channel; those are for temperature and touch respectively.

questions.q: According to place theory, how is sound frequency encoded in the cochlea?
options: By the frequency of action potentials in a single cochlear nerve fiber
By the amplitude of the basilar membrane vibration at one fixed point
By which location along the cochlea is maximally activated, with each place responding to a specific frequency
By the number of outer hair cells that depolarise simultaneously
By the time delay between sound onset and cochlear nerve fiber activation
questions.explain: Place theory states that each location along the cochlea corresponds to a specific sound frequency — the cochlea performs a spatial frequency analysis. Low frequencies activate the apex; high frequencies activate the base. This spatial map was established by Georg von Békésy.

questions.q: Which scientist established place theory and won the Nobel Prize for this work?
options: David Julius
Ardem Patapoutian
Alan Hodgkin
Georg von Békésy
Santiago Ramón y Cajal
questions.explain: Georg von Békésy (1899–1972) established place theory, demonstrating that each location along the cochlea responds maximally to a specific frequency. He won the Nobel Prize for this work. David Julius and Ardem Patapoutian won the 2021 Nobel for TRP channels and Piezo channels.

questions.q: A patient loses Piezo2 function in their peripheral sensory neurons. Which two sensory abilities would be most directly impaired?
options: Cold temperature sensing and pain from heat
Gentle touch and proprioception
Nociception and auditory frequency discrimination
Taste and olfaction
Vision and vestibular balance
questions.explain: Piezo2 mediates gentle touch AND proprioception (the sense of body position and self-movement). Loss of Piezo2 would impair both. TRPV1 and TRPM8 handle temperature; auditory transduction involves a different mechanotransduction channel still under investigation.

questions.q: A researcher finds that applying a compound to OHCs prevents prestin from changing shape in response to voltage. What would be the expected physiological consequence?
options: IHCs would stop releasing glutamate onto cochlear nerve fibers
Cochlear amplification would be abolished, reducing hearing sensitivity
Place frequency coding would shift so all tones are perceived at the cochlear apex
The tip links in OHC stereocilia would break and prevent mechanotransduction
Secondary sensory neurons would fail to cross the midline
questions.explain: Prestin's electromotility drives cochlear amplification in OHCs. If prestin cannot change shape, OHCs cannot amplify basilar membrane vibrations, abolishing cochlear amplification and reducing hearing sensitivity. IHC NT release and tip link function are separate mechanisms; midline crossing is a CNS process.

questions.q: Which of the following is a correct comparison between neural and non-neural sensory receptors?
options: Both fire action potentials directly; they differ only in the type of ion channel used
Neural receptors produce receptor potentials; non-neural receptors produce generator potentials
Neural receptors generate a generator potential and fire their own AP; non-neural receptors generate a receptor potential and release neurotransmitter to drive APs in downstream neurons
Non-neural receptors are faster because they skip the synapse; neural receptors are slower
Both produce graded potentials of equal amplitude regardless of stimulus strength
questions.explain: Neural sensory receptors produce a generator potential that directly triggers an AP in the same cell. Non-neural sensory receptors produce a receptor potential that drives neurotransmitter release, which then generates APs in downstream neurons via synaptic transmission. The terminology is often confused in exams.
